# Self-hosted Live Updates with Azure DevOps - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Overview[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#overview "Direct link to Overview")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Self-hosted Live Updates allows customers to build and host their web artifacts then deploy them as Live Updates to app users. To help ensure secure end-to-end delivery, code signing and signature verification steps are included as well.

This example walks through setting up Self-hosted live updates in a Capacitor app using Azure DevOps.

Requirements[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#requirements "Direct link to Requirements")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

For this example, we've already followed the Self-hosted live update [App Setup instructions](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/self-hosted#app-setup)
. This includes creating an Appflow app, a personal access token (PAT), a public/private key, and configuring the Capacitor app.

Azure DevOps Setup[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#azure-devops-setup "Direct link to Azure DevOps Setup")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

With your Capacitor app configured to use self-hosted live updates, we can configure Azure DevOps next.

### Store the Private Key Securely[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#store-the-private-key-securely "Direct link to Store the Private Key Securely")

To store the private key securely in Azure, upload it to "Secure Files", found under Pipelines -> Library -> Secure files. This also makes it available to the pipeline we'll write shortly.

### Store a Personal Access Token Securely[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#store-a-personal-access-token-securely "Direct link to Store a Personal Access Token Securely")

The personal access token is used to authenticate access to your Appflow account via the Appflow CLI, so it also needs to be made available to the pipeline. Under Pipelines -> Library -> Variable groups, create a new variable group ("Ionic Tokens" for example), then create a variable named APPFLOW\_PAT (or similar). Click the lock icon to mark the variable as secret.

### Create an Azure Storage Blob[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#create-an-azure-storage-blob "Direct link to Create an Azure Storage Blob")

The final Azure setup step involves creating an Azure storage blob to host the live updates. From the [Azure Portal](https://portal.azure.com/)
, navigate to Storage Accounts and create a new one. You may need to create a new Resource group (such as "liveupdates"),

On the next screen, Advanced, turn on "Allow enabling public access on containers." Public access is required for the Capacitor Live Updates plugin to download live updates onto your app user's devices. It's worth noting that by default, Azure encrypts storage account data at rest. Turn on "Require secure transfer for REST API operations" and set Access tier to "Hot", ideal for frequently access data such as live updates.

Once the Storage Account has been created, navigate into it and under Data Storage -> Containers, create a new container ("liveupdates"). This is where the live update artifacts will be stored. Set the Public access level to "Blob (anonymous read access for blobs only)."

Once the container has been created, click into it and navigate to Properties. Note the URL - this is the public URL that we'll register with Appflow. An example URL looks like: [https://ionicselfhosted.blob.core.windows.net/liveupdates](https://ionicselfhosted.blob.core.windows.net/liveupdates)
.

### Write a Pipeline[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#write-a-pipeline "Direct link to Write a Pipeline")

Now, we're ready to write an Azure pipeline that creates and registers a live update. Here's the complete YAML script:

#### Complete YAML Script[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#complete-yaml-script "Direct link to Complete YAML Script")

    # Self-hosted Live Updates pipeline exampletrigger:  - mainpool:  vmImage: ubuntu-latestvariables:  - group: 'Ionic Tokens'  - name: liveUpdateFilepath    value: 'live-update-bundle-$(Build.SourceVersion)'  - name: newLiveUpdateArtifactPath    value: LIVE_UPDATE_CONTAINER/$(liveUpdateFilepath)/live-update-manifest.jsonsteps:  - task: Npm@1    inputs:      command: 'install'      workingDir: '$(Build.SourcesDirectory)'    displayName: 'npm Install'  - script: |      npm run build    workingDirectory: $(Build.SourcesDirectory)    displayName: 'Build Web'  - script: curl -sL https://ionic.io/get-appflow-cli | bash    displayName: 'Install Appflow CLI'  # Download a secure file to the agent machine  - task: DownloadSecureFile@1    name: ionicSigningKey    inputs:      secureFile: 'PEM_FILE'  # Generate Live Update manifest file  - script: |      appflow live-update generate-manifest        --build-dir=DIR        --token=$(APPFLOW_PAT)        --signing-key=$(ionicSigningKey.secureFilePath)    displayName: 'Generate Live Update manifest file'  # Upload the live update to Azure Storage  - task: AzureCLI@2    inputs:      azureSubscription: 'SUBSCRIPTION'      scriptType: pscore      scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'      inlineScript: |        az storage blob upload-batch          --account-name ACCOUNT_NAME          -d liveupdates          --destination-path $(liveUpdateFilepath)          -s www/    displayName: 'Upload all live update files to Azure blob storage'  # Register the signed live update with Appflow  - script: |      appflow live-update register-artifact        --app-id=APP_ID        --artifact-url='$(newLiveUpdateArtifactPath)'        --artifact-type=manifest        --commit-ref=$(Build.SourceBranchName)        --commit-sha=$(Build.SourceVersion)        --commit-message='$(Build.SourceVersionMessage)'        --token=$(APPFLOW_PAT)    displayName: 'Register live update with Appflow'

Let's walk through this pipeline step by step.

#### Pipeline Trigger and VM[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#pipeline-trigger-and-vm "Direct link to Pipeline Trigger and VM")

This pipeline will run every time there is a commit to the "production" branch. We recommend running on a Linux VM, so `ubuntu-latest` is used.

    # Self-hosted Live Updates pipeline exampletrigger:  - productionpool:  vmImage: ubuntu-latest

#### Create Variables[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#create-variables "Direct link to Create Variables")

Next, we'll prepare some variables.

*   `group`: Including the "Ionic Tokens" group makes the personal access token we stored earlier (`APPFLOW_PAT`) accessible to this pipeline.
*   `liveUpdateFilepath`: The filename for the live update that will be created in this pipeline. Adding the Git commit SHA to the end (`Build.SourceVersion`) will make each live update filename unique. `newLiveUpdateArtifactPath`: The URL to the Azure storage blog we created earlier, such as `https://ionicselfhosted.blob.core.windows.net/liveupdates/$(liveUpdateFilepath)/live-update-manifest.json`.

    variables:  - group: 'Ionic Tokens'  - name: liveUpdateFilepath    value: 'live-update-bundle-$(Build.SourceVersion)'  - name: newLiveUpdateArtifactPath    value: LIVE_UPDATE_CONTAINER/$(liveUpdateFilepath)

#### Build the Web App[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#build-the-web-app "Direct link to Build the Web App")

Next, we build the web app. Make any changes needed to build your app.

    steps:  - task: Npm@1    inputs:      command: 'install'      workingDir: '$(Build.SourcesDirectory)'    displayName: 'npm Install'  - script: |      npm run build    workingDirectory: $(Build.SourcesDirectory)    displayName: 'Build Web'

#### Install Appflow CLI[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#install-appflow-cli "Direct link to Install Appflow CLI")

Next, install the Appflow CLI so we can use Self-hosted live update features:

    - script: curl -sL https://ionic.io/get-appflow-cli | bash  displayName: 'Install Appflow CLI'

#### Download Private Key to VM[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#download-private-key-to-vm "Direct link to Download Private Key to VM")

Next, we download the private key file we stored earlier to make it accessible to this pipeline.

*   `secureFile`: The private key pem file, such as `appflow_private.pem`.

    # Download a secure file to the agent machine- task: DownloadSecureFile@1  name: ionicSigningKey # The name with which to reference the secure file's path on the agent  inputs:    secureFile: 'PEM_FILE'

#### Create a Signed Live Update[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#create-a-signed-live-update "Direct link to Create a Signed Live Update")

Now that the web app has been built, we can use the Appflow CLI `generate-manifest` command to create a live update manifest file. The resulting `live-update-manifest.json` file includes a hash for every file in the compiled web app directory. If a native app has a live update manifest file, local files on disk are compared with the manifest and only changed files are downloaded. This makes the update much smaller and faster, resulting in improved performance over slower connections.

*   `build-dir`: The top-level build directory containing the compiled web app, such as `www` for an Angular app.
*   `signing-key`: The private key we retrieved above, used to sign the live update.
*   `token`: The personal access token pulled in from the "Ionic Tokens" variable group above.

    # Bundle the web artifact- script: |    appflow live-update generate-manifest      --build-dir=DIR      --signing-key=$(ionicSigningKey.secureFilePath)      --token=$(APPFLOW_PAT)  displayName: 'Generate Live Update manifest file'

#### Upload Live Update to Azure Blob Storage[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#upload-live-update-to-azure-blob-storage "Direct link to Upload Live Update to Azure Blob Storage")

With the new live update created, we can upload it to Azure blob storage. There are several ways to do so; in this case, we can use the Azure CLI task. We recommend adding this step using the Assistant (search for the "azure cli" task). This example uses the [upload-batch command](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/storage/blob?view=azure-cli-latest#az-storage-blob-upload-batch)
.

*   `azureSubscription`: The subscription name and GUID (will look similar to `'Azure subscription 1(cdc3c210-xxxxxx)'`).
*   `scriptType`: Set to `pscore` (PowerShell Core) and location to `inlineScript`.
*   `inlineScript`: Configure the inline script with various upload parameters. `--account-name` is the storage account name, `-d` is the destination blob container where the files will be uploaded, `--destination-path` is the destination path that will be prepended to the blob name, and `-s` is the source directory where the files to be uploaded are located (`www/` in an Angular app).

    # Upload the live update to Azure Storage- task: AzureCLI@2  inputs:    azureSubscription: 'SUBSCRIPTION'    scriptType: pscore    scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'    inlineScript: |      az storage blob upload-batch        --account-name ACCOUNT_NAME        -d liveupdates        --destination-path $(liveUpdateFilepath)        -s DIR  displayName: 'Upload all live update files to Azure blob storage'

#### Register Signed Live Update with Appflow[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#register-signed-live-update-with-appflow "Direct link to Register Signed Live Update with Appflow")

In this last step, inform Appflow that a new live update is available with the Appflow CLI `register-artifact` command.

*   `app-id`: The Appflow app id, like `042a1261`. Find this value under the app's name in Appflow or the URL (`https://dashboard.ionicframework.com/app/042a1261/getting-started`).
*   `artifact-type`: The type of artifact being registered, either `zip` or `manifest` for differential live updates (recommended).
*   `artifact-url`: The URL where the new live update artifact is stored. The Capacitor live update plugin will download the live update from your storage provider. When using `manifest` artifact-type (recommended), URL should end with `live-update-manifest.json`. When using `zip` artifact-type, URL should end with `.zip`.
*   `token`: The Appflow personal access token.
*   `commit-ref`: The branch name of the commit, such as "main" or "production."
*   `commit-sha`: The Git commit SHA, such as "adf9137y45".
*   `commit-message`: The Git commit message, such as "Fix login bug."

    # Register the signed live update with Appflow- script: |    appflow live-update register-artifact      --app-id=APP_ID      --artifact-type=manifest      --artifact-url='$(newLiveUpdateArtifactPath)'      --commit-ref=$(Build.SourceBranchName)      --commit-sha=$(Build.SourceVersion)      --commit-message='$(Build.SourceVersionMessage)'      --token=$(APPFLOW_PAT)  displayName: 'Register live update with Appflow'

Save and run the pipeline. A Self-hosted live update hosted in Azure is now available!

Deploy a Live Update[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#deploy-a-live-update "Direct link to Deploy a Live Update")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Once the pipeline has finished running, you'll find the new Self-hosted live update in your App's Builds list in Appflow. From there, you can follow the standard Live Update flow of [assigning the build to a Live Update Channel](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/deploy#assign-the-build-to-a-channel)
.

Contents
--------

*   [Azure DevOps Setup](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#azure-devops-setup)
    *   [Store the Private Key Securely](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#store-the-private-key-securely)
        
    *   [Store a Personal Access Token Securely](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops#store-a-personal-access-token-securely)

# Personal Access Tokens - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/personal-access-tokens#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Personal Access Tokens can be used to authenticate the Ionic CLI, [Appflow CLI](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/overview)
 or any third party application.

Personal Access Tokens can be created only for users and not organizations.

### Create a Personal Access Token[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/personal-access-tokens#create-a-personal-access-token "Direct link to Create a Personal Access Token")

To create a Personal Access Token, on the Appflow Dashboard, go to Personal Settings and select the "Personal Access Tokens" tab

![Personal Access Token Tab](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/pat-empty-list-f7cb67a2bc1e260516d67c3c14bcad8b.png)

then click on "Generate new token" and specify a name.

![Personal Access Token New](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/pat-create-new-800c2d08a2d541eba49a54ee810ec658.png)

An expiration date can be chosen if you need the token to stop working after a specific date. If you do not specify a date, the token will never expire.

After clicking "Create token", you will see the new Personal Access Token. Copy the token and make sure you save it: you won’t be able to see it again.

![Personal Access Token List with token](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/pat-list-with-token-265463419130a61f3b160b92813bc71b.png)

### Delete a Personal Access Token[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/personal-access-tokens#delete-a-personal-access-token "Direct link to Delete a Personal Access Token")

To delete a Personal Access Token, go to Personal Settings, select the "Personal Access Tokens" tab and click oo "Delete" button on the row of the token you want to remove.

![Personal Access Token List with token](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/pat-list-without-token-2521dafd80f5dc7b773beda813030523.png)

---

# Manual Deploy - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations/manual#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

To deploy an existing binary a build that has the correct signing certificate selected for the app store destination must be built.

*   From the `Deployment` page `New Deployment` should be selected.

![/img/appflow/appflow-deploy-existing.png](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/appflow-deploy-existing-bd20166d76f71bc4ca203095eec7595d.png)

*   Select the build from the `1. Select build` page
    
*   Choose the destination for the build from the `2. Configure Deployment` page
    
*   The result will be shown in the `Deployment` page
    
*   Once you have returned to the `Deployment` page you can click on the row to see the logs for the Deployment

---

# App Previews - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/intro#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Appflow provides multiple ways to view a build of your application before deploying to users. App previews are useful for testing, QA, or sharing new versions of your app with team members and stakeholders.

Appflow supports in-browser previews for web builds, as well as device previews for web and Android.

##### Helpful links[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/intro#helpful-links "Direct link to Helpful links")

[![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-quickstart-icon.png)![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-quickstart-icon.png)\
\
Preview web builds of your mobile app in Appflow or on your device.](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/web-previews)
[![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-installation-icon.png)![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-installation-icon.png)\
\
Install native builds from Appflow on a mobile device.](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/native-previews)

---

# Builds/Automations - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations/builds#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

A new build, either as the result of a manual build or as a result of an automation, can be deployed to app stores.

Careful selection of build type and signing certificates must be made to produce a build that can be properly uploaded to the app store selected.

![/img/appflow/deploy-to-appstore-builds.png](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/deploy-to-appstore-builds-88a6a069a1cc3b85e233c260a0477ebd.png)

*   Build Type
    
    *   Must be `App Store` or `Enterprise` for iOS Xcode builds and `Release` for Android builds.
*   Signing Certificate
    
    *   The signing certificate must the one that will sign the build properly for uploading to the correct app store.
*   Destinations
    
    *   For Xcode builds you can select Apple App store, for Android builds you can select Google Play Store.

---

# Deploy To App Stores - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations/intro#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Ionic Appflow can deploy builds to the Apple App Store and the Google Play Store, entirely in the cloud. Push changes to app stores from anywhere, on any platform.

The feature is supported in builds created manually from the Appflow Dashboard, Automations, and builds triggered from the Ionic CLI.

##### Helpful links[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations/intro#helpful-links "Direct link to Helpful links")

[![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-quickstart-icon.png)![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-quickstart-icon.png)\
\
Configure Appflow to deploy to the Apple App Store!](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations/apple)
[![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-quickstart-icon.png)![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-quickstart-icon.png)\
\
Configure Appflow to deploy to the Google Play Store!](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations/google)

Contents
--------

---

# Exploring Appflow Tutorial - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Welcome to "Exploring Appflow", a tutorial that teaches you the fundamentals of Appflow.

Appflow is a mobile DevOps solution for teams building apps with any hybrid mobile architecture, including apps built with Cordova, Capacitor, and React Native. It helps teams increase agility, app quality, and time to market by automating key phases of the development lifecycle.

Appflow is easy to set up. In fact, no CI/CD expertise is required. Ready to get started?

---

# Native Previews - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/native-previews#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Previews on Android devices[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/native-previews#previews-on-android-devices "Direct link to Previews on Android devices")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

Install .APK files created by Android debug builds directly on your Android device. Simply scan the QR code provided in Appflow to get started.

Access the QR code for an Android build on the Builds list in Appflow or from the build details page.

![Android Preview Builds List](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-android-preview-builds-list-8172fe2171e2defdc5ef2ab37a5bb813.png)

Scanning the QR code with an Android device will open the `.apk` file to be installed. You may need to bypass a warning or update your settings to allow for third-party app installations on your device.

Native App Previews[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/native-previews#native-app-previews "Direct link to Native App Previews")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

Preview your app directly in the browser via a secure public URL with [Appetize](https://appetize.io/)
. Appflow makes it easy to upload an iOS simulator or Android debug build directly to Appetize.

Important

Native app preview URLs contain unguessable cryptographically generated 128-bit random string (a.k.a. publicKey). By default, anybody who has your app's link, i.e. its publicKey, will have permission to run your app. Use your discretion while sharing this URL externally.

### Building a Native App Preview[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/native-previews#building-a-native-app-preview "Direct link to Building a Native App Preview")

An [Appetize.io](http://appetize.io/)
 powered native app preview can be generated on demand for each build.

When performing a new Build, you should choose "iOS" or "Android" as the target platform, ensure the Build type is "Simulator" (for iOS) or "Debug" (for Android), then toggle "Native app preview" to Enabled.

![Native Preview Build Toggle](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-native-preview-build-toggle-09a46e5ea64efa5f4d40e352f15256ef.png)

After the build succeeds, you'll be able to click the eye icon in your builds list to view that Native app preview:

![Native Preview Build List Link](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-native-preview-build-list-link-5ecc524d282c0efb950480ea49a61a95.png)

See the [Appetize docs](https://docs.appetize.io/features)
 to learn about all the features of Appetize.

Contents
--------

*   [Previews on Android devices](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/native-previews#previews-on-android-devices)
    
*   [Native App Previews](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/native-previews#native-app-previews)
    *   [Building a Native App Preview](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/native-previews#building-a-native-app-preview)

---

# Web Previews - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/web-previews#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

When you're performing a Web Build, you have the opportunity to turn on the "Preview URL" destination. This URL shows a running web version of your application either in a phone "chrome" or full-screen if you open it on a mobile device.

This allows you to quickly look at and share a specific version of your application. Here you can see our demo application (Ionifits) running:

![Web Preview View](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-web-preview-page-a8fdb750234c483fd8e7210a06232dbc.png)

Turning on Preview URLs[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/web-previews#turning-on-preview-urls "Direct link to Turning on Preview URLs")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

All existing applications on Appflow have Preview URLs disabled by default. If you start a new application, you'll have the ability to turn them on as well.

In order to turn URLs on, you'll need to visit the "Web preview" settings inside the dashboard for your app and enable them.

![Web Preview Settings](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-web-preview-settings-ded15f0898726f44a4e7d13648f1ee12.png)

There are a few things to note about Preview URLs:

*   Preview URLs expose a **public URL** that can be accessed by anyone with the URL. The URL is generated using a random preview ID which makes it hard if not impossible for someone to guess.
*   Our Previews are built to expose the Web version of your application. If your app is PWA ready all features should work fine, but if you're utilizing native APIs specifically, those will not function and you might receive errors.
*   Other issues might arise that you'll have to handle inside your app or server (such as CORS). Appflow does not provide support for these issues, but you can inspect the application and see any errors like normal.
*   Preview URLs merely exist to show you your running app and share it with stakeholders, they only last for 7 days, and should not be utilized for long-term hosting.
*   Just enabling Web Previews will not automatically give you a working URL, you must also Build a web preview (see below)

Building a Web Preview[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/web-previews#building-a-web-preview "Direct link to Building a Web Preview")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In order to get a Web Preview, you must enable it for a specific build (either a one-time build or an automation).

When performing a new Build or setting up an Automation, you should choose "Web" as the target platform, then toggle "Web preview" to Enabled.

![Web Preview Build Toggle](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-web-preview-build-toggle-d2c28fdfa597788c5ff64ce4f2962cdb.png)

After the build succeeds, you'll be able to click the eye icon in your builds list to view that Web preview:

![Web Preview Build List Link](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-web-preview-build-list-link-66e28fed1576a8d49404566984f9ae9a.png)

Sharing Preview URLs[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/web-previews#sharing-preview-urls "Direct link to Sharing Preview URLs")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In order to share a Web Preview with someone else, you'll need to send them the public share URL. This can be done by clicking the "Share" button from a Web Preview in the top right.

You can also share the QR code displayed on the Web Preview which can be scanned with the stock Camera application on an iOS or Android device which will open the URL inside the phone's browser.

Preview URL App Mode[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/web-previews#preview-url-app-mode "Direct link to Preview URL App Mode")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can switch between previewing different modes of your application by clicking the "iPhone" dropdown in the top right of the Web preview. This allows you to choose other devices like Android or a tablet.

Contents
--------

*   [Turning on Preview URLs](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/web-previews#turning-on-preview-urls)
    
*   [Building a Web Preview](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/web-previews#building-a-web-preview)
    
*   [Sharing Preview URLs](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/web-previews#sharing-preview-urls)
    
*   [Preview URL App Mode](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/web-previews#preview-url-app-mode)

---

# Alternate Appflow Build Command - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-build#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

For generating the web build Appflow will use the build script defined in the package.json, unless there is a script named `appflow:build` defined.

In the `scripts` section of the package.json for an Application define a `appflow:build` element. It can be any command valid as a [npm script](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v6/using-npm/scripts)
.

For example: package.json

    {  "name": "AppFlowTest",  "version": "0.0.1",  "private": true,  "scripts": {    "appflow:build": "some-build-command",  },...

---

# Live Update Builds - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/builds#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

A Live Update build of your application includes only the web assets (JavaScript, HTML, CSS, fonts, and images) which can be shipped out to customer via a Live Update deployment.

Creating Live Update Web Builds[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/builds#creating-live-update-web-builds "Direct link to Creating Live Update Web Builds")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Create a Live Update web build manually from the Ionic Dashboard, in the **Builds** section under **Build** in the sidebar. Next, choose the code commit to build. After picking the commit, select `Web` from the `Target platform` dropdown menu and choose the preferred [build stack](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/build-stacks)
.

![Navigating to Live Update Builds](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/deploy-builds-create-location-15c4ca1ce97f15a42f0854d6f30fdf36.png)

A Live Update build will do the following:

*   Run `npm install` to install all web project dependencies.
*   Run `npm run build` if a `build` script is detected in the `scripts` section of your `package.json` in order to build the web assets for your application. The `www` directory (or the directory specified in the `capacitor.config.ts` using the key `webDir` if it is a Capacitor project) containing your built application will be stored and made available for live updates.
*   Generate a Live Update manifest file. This includes a hash of every file in the built web directory. After a Live Update is deployed, the manifest file contained within the native app is compared against the manifest in the Live Update. Only the modified files are downloaded (also known as [differential live updates](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/differentials)
    ), making the Live Update small and performant.

Find the history and status of your Live Update builds in the side menu under `Builds` in the `Build` tab.

Set Up Channels[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/builds#set-up-channels "Direct link to Set Up Channels")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A Live Update channel points to a specific web build of your app that will be shared with any devices configured to listen to that channel for updates. Next, let's set up your channels.

Contents
--------

*   [Creating Live Update Web Builds](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/builds#creating-live-update-web-builds)
    
*   [Set Up Channels](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/builds#set-up-channels)

---

# Destinations - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Destinations represent services where you can send your build artifacts (Web Assets, IPAs, or APKs/AABs). You can create one by navigating to the `Live Update Channels` or `Store Destinations` tabs located under the `Deployments` tab.

![/img/appflow/deploy-to-appstore-destinations-tab.png](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/deploy-to-appstore-destinations-tab-6ccff5aab3fc105ccc0766186af40bd6.png)

Live Update Channels[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations#live-update-channels "Direct link to Live Update Channels")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Live Update Channels](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels)
 are destinations used to deploy real time changes to your app using [Appflow's live update feature](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/intro)
. By default, a Production channel is created for you and can't be deleted.

To create a new channel click the `New Channel` button in the top right and then enter the name for the new channel.

![/img/appflow/create-new-live-update-channel.png](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/create-new-live-update-channel-516cb142eae6ec964f306bb91bcd5cb3.png)

Store Destinations[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations#store-destinations "Direct link to Store Destinations")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Store Destinations defines an [Apple App Store](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations/apple)
 or [Google Play Store](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations/google)
 location where native build artifacts are uploaded

To begin creating a new store destination, click the `New Store Destination` button in the top right and then choose from either `Apple App Store` or `Google Play` from the dropdown.

![/img/appflow/apple-add-destination.png](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/apple-add-destination-09da19f7fdeb5313534ea90143cfaf0c.png)

Contents
--------

*   [Live Update Channels](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations#live-update-channels)
    
*   [Store Destinations](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations#store-destinations)

---

# Apple App Store - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations/apple#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

The Apple App Store destination allows you to upload your finished binaries to the App Store Connect service for iOS devices.

![/img/appflow/apple-add-destination.png](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/apple-add-destination-09da19f7fdeb5313534ea90143cfaf0c.png)

For this destination you will need the following:

Prerequisite

In order to build a binary that you can upload to the Apple App Store you will need a production signing certificate configured in Appflow, details [here](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#ios-credentials)

*   **A name for instance of the Apple App Store destination**
    
    *   Something that users will refer to this configuration as
*   **Your Apple ID**
    
    *   Does not need to be the owner account, but does need to be an account that can upload binaries and update the app
*   **An App-specific password for your Apple ID**
    
    *   To set up an App-Specific Password you need to enable two-step verification for your account.
    *   Once that is enabled, sign in to [https://appleid.apple.com/](https://appleid.apple.com/)
         and in the Security Section there is an option to create/manage App-Specific Passwords
*   **The Apple ID for the App from App Store Connect**
    
    *   This is a value generated by Apple that is unique to your app. To find it :
        1.  Log into App Store Connect: [https://appstoreconnect.apple.com/](https://appstoreconnect.apple.com/)
            
        2.  Navigate to the App
        3.  On the page for the App, Under General Information, is the Apple ID (should be all numbers)
    
    ![/img/appflow/apple-apple-id.png](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/apple-apple-id-bb686ba5c244bd3db9e7a65cc1c7a9b6.png)
    
*   **Team ID for the App from App Store Connect**
    
    *   You can find this value in the [Membership Details](https://developer.apple.com/account/#membership-details)
         page in your Apple Developer account.

---

# Run Scripts During The Build - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/hooks#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

During the Appflow build process, it might be necessary to run custom scripts and it is important to know what will and won't run.

### Capacitor Hooks[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/hooks#capacitor-hooks "Direct link to Capacitor Hooks")

[Capacitor hooks](https://capacitorjs.com/docs/cli/hooks)
 are specified in your `package.json` to execute scripts around the execution of capacitor commands. Appflow specifically executes `npx cap sync` for native builds so the `capacitor:sync:before` and `capacitor:sync:after` hooks can be used in the Appflow build process.

### Cordova Hooks[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/hooks#cordova-hooks "Direct link to Cordova Hooks")

Cordova hooks are specified in your `config.xml` and when used with the Cordova CLI will execute scripts. Appflow however uses Gym to compile your Application, so Cordova hooks **will not** run with the exception of `after_platform_add` and `after_prepare`.

### Ionic Hooks[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/hooks#ionic-hooks "Direct link to Ionic Hooks")

Ionic has a set of [hooks](https://ionicframework.com/docs/cli/configuration#hooks)
 which are specified in your `package.json` to execute scripts during certain events. These hooks **do not** run with Appflow.

### Npm Pre & Post Scripts[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/hooks#npm-pre--post-scripts "Direct link to Npm Pre & Post Scripts")

Npm will execute scripts before and after a command is run (documented [here](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v7/using-npm/scripts#pre--post-scripts)
). If you create a script called `prebuild` or `postbuild` in your `package.json` these will run before and after the `build` step in Appflow.

note

The `postbuild` script of `package.json` will run after your web source code is compiled and not after your native code is compiled.

### Conclusion[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/hooks#conclusion "Direct link to Conclusion")

If you are looking to run scripts during the Appflow build process you can use npm pre & post scripts, the `after_platform_add` and `after_prepare` hooks for cordova projects, and the `capacitor:sync:before` and `capacitor:sync:after` hooks for Capacitor projects. For additional automation options look at the Appflow [Automation](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/automation/intro)
 feature.

Contents
--------

*   [Capacitor Hooks](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/hooks#capacitor-hooks)
    
*   [Cordova Hooks](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/hooks#cordova-hooks)
    
*   [Ionic Hooks](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/hooks#ionic-hooks)
    
*   [Npm Pre & Post Scripts](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/hooks#npm-pre--post-scripts)
    
*   [Conclusion](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/hooks#conclusion)

---

# Instantly Update App UI & Fix Bugs | Appflow Live Updates

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/intro#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

Use Appflow's Live Updates feature to update the UI and business logic of your app remotely, in real-time. Push HTML, JS, and CSS updates directly to your users without going through the app store to instantly fix bugs and ship new features.

note

The Live Updates feature only works on binary compatible changes (HTML, CSS, and JS). If you change native code, such as adding or removing a plugin or updating the Android or iOS project files, then you must resubmit a new [native binary build](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/intro)
 to the app stores.

##### Helpful links[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/intro#helpful-links "Direct link to Helpful links")

[![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-installation-icon.png)![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-installation-icon.png)\
\
Get started by installing and configuring the Live Updates SDK.](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/capacitor-sdk)
[![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-quickstart-icon.png)![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-quickstart-icon.png)\
\
Quickly ship your first Live Update!](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update)
[![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-faq-icon.png)![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-faq-icon.png)\
\
Find answers to common questions about Live Updates.](https://ionic.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/categories/360000410474-Deploy-Builds-Git-)
[![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-faq-icon.png)![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-faq-icon.png)\
\
Find answers to common questions about Live Updates.](https://ionic.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/categories/360000409113-Deploy)
[![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-deploy-icon.png)![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-deploy-icon.png)\
\

# Channels - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

A Live Update channel points to a specific web build of your app that will be shared with any devices configured to listen to that channel for updates. When you [install the Live Updates SDK](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/capacitor-sdk)
 in your app, any native binary configured to that channel will check the channel for available updates whenever the app is first opened. You can change the build a channel points to whenever you'd like and can rollback changes as well.

Setting up a Channel[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels#setting-up-a-channel "Direct link to Setting up a Channel")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Every app comes with the default channel **Production** (this cannot be deleted). To add new channels, go to Deploy -> Destinations, and then click the `New destination` button. Choose type `Live Update Channel` then enter a channel name.

Channel names can be anything you'd like. A common strategy is to match channels to your dev environments, such as Development, QA, Staging, and Production. For example, "Development" when trying out Live Updates on a local device or emulator, "QA" for your QA team, and "Production" for the app store binaries your users will interact with.

Configuring the Channel in Your App[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels#configuring-the-channel-in-your-app "Direct link to Configuring the Channel in Your App")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

With several channels created, configure your app to listen to one of them. In this example, we'll use `development`.

### Capacitor SDK[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels#capacitor-sdk "Direct link to Capacitor SDK")

Open the `capacitor.config.ts` (or `capacitor.config.json`) file. Under the `plugins` then `LiveUpdates` configuration section, set the channel to `development`:

    const config: CapacitorConfig = {  plugins: {    LiveUpdates: {      channel: 'development',    }  }};

Next, build the web app then run `npx cap sync` to copy the Capacitor config file into the iOS and Android projects. Note that if you don't do this, the native mobile projects will still point to the previously configured Live Updates channel!

### Cordova SDK[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels#cordova-sdk "Direct link to Cordova SDK")

Click **Install Instructions** next to the `development` channel to view the Ionic CLI command to run. You'll notice the command includes `--channel-name="development"`.

Assigning a Build to a Channel[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels#assigning-a-build-to-a-channel "Direct link to Assigning a Build to a Channel")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Once you have created a Channel, go back to the **Builds** section of your app. On the right side of each Web Build, click the 3 dot ellipsis, then "Deploy Live Update" menu item.

Select the Live Update channel from the dropdown then click the Deploy button. The Live Update becomes active immediately. Once a Web Build is set as active for a Channel, any native binaries that have been configured to watch the channel will receive the new update the next time the app checks in.

note

If a channel referenced in an app using live updates is deleted, live updates will no longer be applied. Previously, the Production channel would be used as a fallback. To reapply live updates, either recreate the same channel name or create then set a new channel name in the app.

Roll Back a Live Update[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels#roll-back-a-live-update "Direct link to Roll Back a Live Update")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Rolling back a Live Update enables you to quickly revert the application to a previously successful deployment. This is useful when you determine the Live Update build has a bug, was accidentally deployed, etc.

From the Destinations page, select the name of the Channel you want to roll back. Click "Roll back to here" on the build you want to roll back to. After completing these steps, the selected build will update and immediately become the Active build.

Automating Deployment from a Git Branch[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels#automating-deployment-from-a-git-branch "Direct link to Automating Deployment from a Git Branch")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you're interested in automating your Live Updates deployments from git push all the way to the app stores, you can take advantage of our [Automation](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/automation/intro)
 feature.

Deploy a Live Update to a Device[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels#deploy-a-live-update-to-a-device "Direct link to Deploy a Live Update to a Device")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Now that we've [installed the Live Update SDK](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/capacitor-sdk)
 and understand how web builds and channels work, we'll walk through the process of deploying a live update to a device.

Contents
--------

*   [Setting up a Channel](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels#setting-up-a-channel)
    
*   [Configuring the Channel in Your App](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels#configuring-the-channel-in-your-app)
    *   [Capacitor SDK](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels#capacitor-sdk)
        
    *   [Cordova SDK](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels#cordova-sdk)
        
*   [Assigning a Build to a Channel](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels#assigning-a-build-to-a-channel)
    
*   [Roll Back a Live Update](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels#roll-back-a-live-update)
    
*   [Automating Deployment from a Git Branch](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels#automating-deployment-from-a-git-branch)
    
*   [Deploy a Live Update to a Device](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels#deploy-a-live-update-to-a-device)

# Google Play Store - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations/google#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

The Google Play destination allows you to upload your finished binaries to the store for Android.

Add Destination[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations/google#add-destination "Direct link to Add Destination")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

note

The first upload for a new Android app **must** be [uploaded from the Google Play Console](https://support.google.com/googleplay/android-developer/answer/9859152)
.

![/img/appflow/google-overview.png](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/google-overview-c0f62598d5383f11b605cf43b613d080.png)

*   **Track**
    *   Which track on the Google Play Store the app will be uploaded into.
*   **Package name**
    *   in reverse domain name format.
        *   e.g. com.yourdomain.yourappname
*   **Publishing Format**
    *   The type of Android build artifact you would like to deploy to Google Play.
        *   Android App Bundles (AAB)
        *   APK

note

As of August 2021, new apps are required to publish with the Android App Bundle (AAB) on Google Play.

To upload Android App Bundles (AAB), additional configuration is needed in the Google Play Console for the app. In particular, you will need to enroll for Play App Signing, otherwise, you cannot upload your app bundle to the Play Console.

For further information please see the [Android Docs](https://developer.android.com/guide/app-bundle)

*   **JSON key**
    *   To upload to the Google Play Store you will need to create a service account and download the JSON file containing security info for the service account.
        1.  Go to Service accounts in the [Google Cloud Platform](https://console.cloud.google.com/iam-admin/serviceaccounts)
            
        2.  Click the **CREATE SERVICE ACCOUNT** button at the top of the **Google Cloud Platform** ![/img/appflow/gcp-create-service-account.png](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/gcp-create-service-account-da32a4e28597a033438baed89f53f79e.png)
        3.  Under the **Service account details** section, enter a `service account name` and click the **CREATE AND CONTINUE** button. ![/img/appflow/gcp-service-account-details.png](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/gcp-service-account-details-b95cbdd02ffff206210c08fb10c7e4e5.png)
        4.  Under the **Select a role** drop down in the **Grant this service account access to project** section, select `Service Accounts` > `Service Account User` and press the **CONTINUE** button. ![/img/appflow/gcp-grant-service-account-access.png](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/gcp-grant-service-account-access-1a8ec96256b9e0293be717789b70351e.png)
        5.  Skip the **Grant users access to this service account** section and press the **DONE** button. This will create the service account which will appear as an item in the service account table.
        6.  Find the newly created service account in this table and select the **Manage Keys** option under the **Actions** column of the table. ![/img/appflow/gcp-manage-keys.png](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/gcp-manage-keys-b87c0929c9ffc8b144d61dd2cf2a4968.png)
        7.  Under the **Keys** section, select the `Create new key` option under the **ADD KEY** drop-down. ![/img/appflow/gcp-create-new-key.png](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/gcp-create-new-key-b1fe6ffe79b233e6a90d60a585f8a537.png)
        8.  Select the `JSON` key type option in the pop up that appears and then press the **CREATE** button. This will create a .json file containing a private key that will be automatically downloaded to your computer. ![/img/appflow/gcp-create-json-key.png](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/gcp-create-json-key-865a2babb3527b8671f77b0e88a5642e.png)

Play App Signing Key[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations/google#play-app-signing-key "Direct link to Play App Signing Key")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Play App Signing by Google Play is required to publish `.aab` files to the Play Store. The Google Play App Signing Key is configured in the Google Play Console, not in Appflow. You can learn more about Play App Signing in the [Android documentation here](https://developer.android.com/studio/publish/app-signing#enroll)
.

Contents
--------

*   [Add Destination](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations/google#add-destination)
    
*   [Play App Signing Key](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/destinations/google#play-app-signing-key)

# FAQ - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Answers to common Live Updates questions.

General[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#general "Direct link to General")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### What is the definition of a "live update"?[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#what-is-the-definition-of-a-live-update "Direct link to What is the definition of a "live update"?")

A live update is a single download of web resources from the Live Updates service. These web resources are used to replace some or all of the contents of either a standalone web app or the contents of a web app hosted within a native app. An app can receive a live update based on:

*   The `CHANNEL_NAME` variable configured in your app's installation of the Live Updates plugin.
*   The native versioning setup for the Web Deploy Build

### What happens when Live Updates downloads are depleted or MAU limit is reached?[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#what-happens-when-live-updates-downloads-are-depleted-or-mau-limit-is-reached "Direct link to What happens when Live Updates downloads are depleted or MAU limit is reached?")

The limits associated with each plan are on a calendar month basis. Your quota will refresh at the beginning of each calendar month. If you exceed your quota for a given month, you will have to upgrade to a higher plan, or wait until the next calendar month to send more live updates.

How do I update the Live Updates plugin?[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#how-do-i-update-the-live-updates-plugin "Direct link to How do I update the Live Updates plugin?")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

New releases of the Live Updates plugin are created to accommodate changes such as bug fixes, new features, and support for new versions of iOS and Android. Changes introduced in new releases modify the native layer of the application, so an app can only receive the latest version of the plugin through a binary app store release.

To update the plugin in the app, install it using `@latest`:

    # Capacitor SDKnpm install @capacitor/live-updates@latest# Cordova SDKnpm install cordova-plugin-ionic@latest

This modifies the `package.json` file to target the latest version of the plugin:

    # Capacitor SDK"@capacitor/live-updates": "^X.X.X",# Cordova SDK"cordova-plugin-ionic": "^X.X.X",

Next, run `npm i` to update `package-lock.json` accordingly. Commit then push changes to the remote repo.

If Appflow is used to create the native build artifacts for the app, then create and deploy new native builds from the Appflow dashboard using the recently pushed commit. Otherwise, prepare the project locally by running the following commands:

    npm run build# Capacitor SDKappflow live-update generate-manifest# Cordova SDKionic deploy manifestnpx cap sync

Finally, compile the project from Android Studio and XCode and deploy the resulting build artifacts to the corresponding app stores.

Monthly Active User (MAU) Tracking[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#monthly-active-user-mau-tracking "Direct link to Monthly Active User (MAU) Tracking")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### How are monthly active users tracked?[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#how-are-monthly-active-users-tracked "Direct link to How are monthly active users tracked?")

On a per-app installation basis. When a request is sent by the Live Updates plugin to Appflow's servers checking for a new Live Update, a unique GUID is sent representing one "user." The number of unique ids is counted up per billing cycle. View the total MAUs counted across all apps on the Usage page under Settings.

### When did MAU data tracking begin?[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#when-did-mau-data-tracking-begin "Direct link to When did MAU data tracking begin?")

Tracking began on March 20th, 2023, making April 2023 the first complete month of data available. Tracking includes customers using the Cordova-based Live Updates plugin and the Capacitor-based Live Updates plugin.

### How accurate is the MAU tracking?[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#how-accurate-is-the-mau-tracking "Direct link to How accurate is the MAU tracking?")

Highly accurate with a margin of error of plus or minus 0.81%. For your benefit, Appflow under reports MAUs by tweaking the algorithm to -1.62% (**minus** 1.62%). For example, if Appflow reports 10,000 MAUs, then the actual range is between 9,838 and 10,000 users.

Installing an app on multiple devices or reinstalling an app can inflate the MAU count.

### Is any PII included?[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#is-any-pii-included "Direct link to Is any PII included?")

No. Only a device-generated Id is sent to Appflow's servers.

Download Tracking[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#download-tracking "Direct link to Download Tracking")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A live update is counted when a download attempt for an update has been triggered. If the "auto" or "background" live update method is used in an app, then a download attempt will occur when the app is opened and after the app confirms that a compatible live update is available for download.

An app using the "background" live update method can also trigger a download attempt once the app has regained focus after being in the background for longer than the duration specified by the `minBackgroundDuration` configuration.

If the download of the live update is interrupted (e.g. due to unstable internet connectivity) then the live update will not be applied to the app but the download attempt will count towards the live update usage.

View the total downloads counted across all apps on the Usage page under Settings.

Contents
--------

*   [General](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#general)
    *   [What is the definition of a "live update"?](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#what-is-the-definition-of-a-live-update)
        
    *   [What happens when Live Updates downloads are depleted or MAU limit is reached?](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#what-happens-when-live-updates-downloads-are-depleted-or-mau-limit-is-reached)
        
*   [How do I update the Live Updates plugin?](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#how-do-i-update-the-live-updates-plugin)
    
*   [Monthly Active User (MAU) Tracking](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#monthly-active-user-mau-tracking)
    *   [How are monthly active users tracked?](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#how-are-monthly-active-users-tracked)
        
    *   [When did MAU data tracking begin?](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/faq#when-did-mau-data-tracking-begin)
