# Auto-incrementing Build Numbers in Capacitor - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/auto-incrementing-build-numbers#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Appflow provides an auto-incrementing environment variable (`CI_BUILD_NUMBER`) that can be used with [Trapeze](https://trapeze.dev/)
 to increment build numbers for the native builds of Capacitor apps.

note

An app demonstrating this feature can be found [here](https://github.com/ionic-team/demo-appflow-auto-increment)
.

To use this feature, first install `@trapezedev/configure` project into your app:

    npm install @trapezedev/configure

note

Platform flags (`--ios` and `--android`) are only available in Trapeze v7.0.7+.

Next, create a simple `appflow.yml` file with the following contents:

    vars:  CI_BUILD_NUMBER:    default: 1platforms:  ios:    buildNumber: $CI_BUILD_NUMBER  android:    versionCode: $CI_BUILD_NUMBER

Finally, add the `appflow:build` script to your npm scripts in `package.json` to run this configuration during an Appflow build:

      "scripts": {    "appflow:build": "if [ \"$CI_PLATFORM\" != \"web\" ]; then npx trapeze run appflow.yml -y --$CI_PLATFORM; fi && npm run build",  },

The Appflow-provided `$CI_PLATFORM` environment variable dynamically resolves to the platform name of the currently running build (either `ios` or `android`). The `--ios` and `--android` flags tell Trapeze to run the specified platform only. Since Appflow only builds one platform at a time, combining these features effectively allows us to use one Trapeze script containing both iOS and Android commands in Appflow.

Make sure to keep your build command as the second part of the `appflow:build` script to ensure your app web assets build properly.

Setting Initial Build Number[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/auto-incrementing-build-numbers#setting-initial-build-number "Direct link to Setting Initial Build Number")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you'd like to start incrementing at a certain number, use the following `appflow:build` command instead, substituting your initial build number for the number `1234`. All subsequent build numbers will be added to this number:

      "scripts": {    "appflow:build": "CI_BUILD_NUMBER=$(($CI_BUILD_NUMBER + 1234)); if [ \"$CI_PLATFORM\" != \"web\" ]; then npx trapeze run appflow.yml -y --$CI_PLATFORM; fi && npm run build"  },

Learn more[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/auto-incrementing-build-numbers#learn-more "Direct link to Learn more")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Trapeze](https://trapeze.dev/)
 has a number of additional features that may be useful in your build pipeline. Explore the [ionic-team/trapeze](https://github.com/ionic-team/trapeze)
 repo to learn more.

Contents
--------

*   [Setting Initial Build Number](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/auto-incrementing-build-numbers#setting-initial-build-number)
    
*   [Learn more](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/auto-incrementing-build-numbers#learn-more)

---

# Override Java version on Android builds - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/override-java-version#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

Starting with the build stack `Linux 2021.09` Appflow tries to detect which version of the Android Gradle Plugin is used in the project.

Starting with build stack `Linux 2025.01`, Java 21 was added to support Capacitor 7 projects.

Here is how the Gradle Plugin version maps to the Java version used:

| Gradle Plugin Version | Java Version |
| --- | --- |
| <= 6 | 8   |
| 7   | 11  |
| 8.0.0 - 8.7.1 | 17  |
| <= 8.7.2 | 21\* |

note

For Gradle Plugin versions >= 8.7.2, Java 21 is recommended and will be used if available. Systems will automatically fall back to Java 17 if Java 21 is not available

If a specific version of Java is needed, it is possible to override the automatic detection using `OVERRIDE_JAVA_VERSION` environment variable.

![Use different Java](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/override-java-version-667bec09cf9cb907fb3e0f74cec9b963.png)

#### Notes[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/override-java-version#notes "Direct link to Notes")

The environment variable `OVERRIDE_JAVA_VERSION` can only be set to an integer representing the major version, such as `17`. Even if `17.0.6` is a valid version, it is not a valid input.

If `OVERRIDE_JAVA_VERSION` is set to an invalid or unavailable version the build will fail.

note

Always refer to the [Build Stacks page](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/build-stacks)
 for the most up to date values.

Contents
--------

---

# Using private Git repositories - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private_git#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

During the build process, it might be necessary to access other private repositories containing, for instance, libraries.

In case such repositories do not belong to the same user that can clone the main application codebase, it is possible to use git credentials codebase.

### How to use Git Credentials[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private_git#how-to-use-git-credentials "Direct link to How to use Git Credentials")

The first step is to generate a personal access token: see the [github](https://help.github.com/articles/creating-a-personal-access-token-for-the-command-line/)
 or [bitbucket](https://confluence.atlassian.com/bitbucket/app-passwords-828781300.html)
 documentation.

Then build an url for the Git Credentials.

The URL for GitHub looks like:

    https://<username>:<token>@github.com

while for Bitbucket looks like:

    https://<username>:<token>@bitbucket.org

and GitLab looks like:

    https://<username>:<token>@gitlab.com

where the `username` is the username of the account used to generate the token.

note

You cannot add Git credentials for the same provider that your repository is hosted on. For instance, if your repository is hosted on GitHub and you add additional Git credentials for a GitHub user then the additional credentials will be ignored.

Finally add a secret inside an [environment](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/environments#custom-environments)
 with key called `GIT_CREDENTIALS` and for the value use the url created before.

![Git credentials token secret](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/git-credentials-token-secret-19e28d0bf7974cb7623d6c1fefa3acd6.png)

At this point, if you use this environment during a build, you can access your repository specifying the `https` clone url without any token. For instance, if your private repo contains an npm library, the `package.json` would reference it in this way:

    {  ...  "dependencies": {    "my_super_secret_library": "git+https://bitbucket.org/myorg/my_super_secret_library.git",    ...,  }  ...}

Contents
--------

*   [How to use Git Credentials](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private_git#how-to-use-git-credentials)

---

# Adding Certificates - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/adding-credentials#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

After you have generated your Signing Certificate, you must upload it to Appflow.

Uploading Certificates[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/adding-credentials#uploading-certificates "Direct link to Uploading Certificates")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Navigate to your App, then to the `Build > Signing Certificates` in the sidebar.

Click the `Add certificate` button to create a new signing certificate. Choose either iOS or Android credentials and give your certificate a name.

![Add Profile](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-add-profile-edf4ce2c716315e8f5ade201d8c60287.png)

note

Signing certificates are created as either `Production` or `Development` and can only house the [build types](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/build-types)
 associated with the signing certificate type. Appflow will designate the signing certificate type based on the uploaded credentials.

Choose the [certificate files you generated](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials)
 and upload them for each platform, adding any passwords needed to access the certificates.

For iOS, use [the generated](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials)
 `.p12` file for the certificate, the `.mobileprovision` file for the Provisioning Profile, and the password used to generate the `.p12` file for the password.

![Add iOS cert](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ios-add-cert-a2c3554ec8254da7e2d9c60784046d27.png)

For Android, use the generated `.keystore` or `.jks` keystore file and the keystore certificate password, key alias, and key password used to generate the file.

![Add Android cert](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/android-add-cert-b15435e6f8abfc6b911b784b3dfdb88e.png)

Once that signing certificate has been created, can click the `Edit` button to remove and/or upload your iOS and Android certs.

Contents
--------

*   [Uploading Certificates](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/adding-credentials#uploading-certificates)

---

# Making Live Updates Faster - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/differentials#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

Live Updates are smaller and faster if they include only the modified files, or differentials, from previous updates. This is accomplished via a manifest file.

Why do I need manifest file?[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/differentials#why-do-i-need-manifest-file "Direct link to Why do I need manifest file?")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A Live Updates manifest file includes a hash for every file in the built web directory (`www` for Angular apps or `dist` for React apps for example). When a native iOS or Android build is created in Appflow, a manifest file is automatically created for the web portion of the app. When you send out a new Live Update to end user devices, the manifest file embedded in the native app is compared to the manifest in the Live Update. Only changed files will be downloaded.

This makes the Live Update much smaller and faster, resulting in improved performance over slower connections.

If there isn't a manifest file in the bundled web app, all files will be downloaded, resulting in unnecessarily slower updates. In the Cordova-based SDK, this file is called `pro-manifest.json`. The Capacitor-based SDK uses `live-update-manifest.json`.

How do I generate the manifest file?[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/differentials#how-do-i-generate-the-manifest-file "Direct link to How do I generate the manifest file?")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### If using Appflow for Native Builds[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/differentials#if-using-appflow-for-native-builds "Direct link to If using Appflow for Native Builds")

*   If you use Appflow to build the native binaries, Appflow creates the manifest file for you automatically and includes it in the static files. This leads to better subsequent deploys.
*   This can be seen in Appflow right after the `npm run build` step alongside `ionic info` and before `step: cap_sync`. Both Live Updates manifest files are created to ensure a [smooth migration](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog)
     from the Cordova SDK to the Capacitor one.

     Generating app manifest... $ ionic live-update manifest ▸ [OK] Appflow Deploy manifest written to ./www/live-update-manifest.json!

### If using Cordova and not using Appflow for Native Builds[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/differentials#if-using-cordova-and-not-using-appflow-for-native-builds "Direct link to If using Cordova and not using Appflow for Native Builds")

*   The manifest should be generated automatically on the `cordova prepare` event (Cordova hooks). No additional action required.
    
*   Since Cordova takes care of this, any subsequent Live Updates to the native build should download only the differentials.
    

### If using Capacitor and not using Appflow for Native Builds[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/differentials#if-using-capacitor-and-not-using-appflow-for-native-builds "Direct link to If using Capacitor and not using Appflow for Native Builds")

You are required to generate the manifest files manually for [Capacitor](https://capacitorjs.com/docs)
 apps.

**If you use the Cordova SDK** (`cordova-plugin-ionic`), run command `ionic live-update manifest`.

    ionic live-update manifest[OK] Appflow Deploy manifest written to ./www/pro-manifest.json!

**If you use the Capacitor SDK** (`@capacitor/live-updates`), run:

    # Install Appflow CLIcurl -fsSL https://ionic.io/get-appflow-cli | bash# Generate manifest. WEB DIR = www, dist, etc.appflow live-update generate-manifest --dir=[WEB DIR]`

Since the manifest file needs to be created after you have modified the web assets, you need to run `npm run build` after creating the web assets and before you copy or update the plugins and platforms using `npx cap sync` or `npx cap copy`.

    npm run buildionic live-update manifestnpx cap sync

The simplest way to manage that locally is by adding it to the build script in `package.json`.

     "scripts": {   "ng": "ng",   "start": "ng serve",   "build": "ng build; ionic live-update manifest",  },

note

There can be issues when the manifest is prepared on Windows due to how line endings are handled and committed. If you're building for native on Windows you may want to try a build using Appflow and see if you notice an improvement.

How do I know if my app contains a manifest file?[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/differentials#how-do-i-know-if-my-app-contains-a-manifest-file "Direct link to How do I know if my app contains a manifest file?")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can verify the Live Updates manifest file by extracting the production `.ipa/.apk` file and checking for the presence of `pro-manifest.json` or `live-update-manifest.json` in the web directory of choice.

Contents
--------

*   [Why do I need manifest file?](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/differentials#why-do-i-need-manifest-file)
    
*   [How do I generate the manifest file?](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/differentials#how-do-i-generate-the-manifest-file)
    *   [If using Appflow for Native Builds](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/differentials#if-using-appflow-for-native-builds)
        
    *   [If using Cordova and not using Appflow for Native Builds](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/differentials#if-using-cordova-and-not-using-appflow-for-native-builds)
        
    *   [If using Capacitor and not using Appflow for Native Builds](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/differentials#if-using-capacitor-and-not-using-appflow-for-native-builds)
        
*   [How do I know if my app contains a manifest file?](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/differentials#how-do-i-know-if-my-app-contains-a-manifest-file)

---

# Automations - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/automations#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Now that you are well versed in triggering different build types in Appflow, it's time to automate builds.

Automations enable you and your team to utilize Appflow's full CI/CD powers. You can create automations that trigger native builds and web builds each time your team checks in new code to a given branch. You can even configure them to use different environments and native configurations so that you can build different versions of your app for development, staging, and production.

### Creating an Automation[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/automations#creating-an-automation "Direct link to Creating an Automation")

Let's create an automation that triggers an Android debug build each time you push code to the main git branch. To get started, navigate to the `Automate` tab within the starter app and click the `New Automation` button in the top right.

![Appflow-new-automation](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/new-automation-56c517a608962f2e07a4762634ace419.png)

Next, you can customize the automation using the fields below:

*   **Name:** A unique name for the automation.
*   **Git Branch:** The branch you'd like to trigger the automation from (ex: main). [See more here](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/automation/create#note-about-git-branch-naming)
    .
*   **Automation Type:** Since we are automating an Android app, select native build.
*   **Build Stack:** You can configure your automations to always use the latest stack or a specific stack. [See more here](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/build-stacks)
    .
*   **Environments:** You can include any previously created environments.
*   **Native configuration:** You can include any previously created native configuration (for example, the previously created QA config).

![Appflow-automation-config](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/automation-config-838468b3fc2011b585d022762173e72f.png)

### Triggering an Automation[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/automations#triggering-an-automation "Direct link to Triggering an Automation")

Now that the automation is active, anytime a developer pushes to the main branch, a new Android debug build will automatically start.

Try making a trivial change and pushing the changes to your git repository. The automation should trigger a new build.

You can also view all the builds associated with a particular automation by navigating to the Automations page in the Appflow dashboard and clicking the appropriate automation from the list.

![Appflow-automation-history](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/automation-history-9d570be8bc3fe9310b1d68968263fa22.png)

### Deployments[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/automations#deployments "Direct link to Deployments")

Automations can also be leveraged to build and deploy Live Updates (covered in the next section) and ship binaries to the appropriate destinations.

![Appflow-web-automation-config](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/web-automation-config-6ada22ddb636eb039994f3aead107eed.png)

Contents
--------

*   [Creating an Automation](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/automations#creating-an-automation)
    
*   [Triggering an Automation](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/automations#triggering-an-automation)
    
*   [Deployments](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/automations#deployments)

---

# Native Builds - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/android#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

In this step, we'll create a debug Android build. This is the easiest option to get started with native builds, since unlike iOS, a signing certificate is not required.

note

Check out [this branch](https://github.com/ionic-team/tutorial-appflow-demo/tree/2_android_builds)
 from this tutorial's companion app to compare your starter project.

### Build starter app[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/android#build-starter-app "Direct link to Build starter app")

You must build your Ionic project at least once before adding any native platforms. To build the web assets of your starter app use the command below:

    ionic build

This creates the `www` folder that Capacitor has been automatically configured to use as the `webDir` in **`capacitor.config.json`**. Learn more about [building your Ionic app here](https://capacitorjs.com/docs/getting-started/with-ionic#build-your-ionic-app)
.

### Package for Native[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/android#package-for-native "Direct link to Package for Native")

To package your starter app for Android native using Capacitor, use the command to add the Android platform:

    npm install @capacitor/androidnpx cap add android

This creates an `android/` directory in the root of the project.

Capacitor projects include the native platform as a source artifact. These are entirely separate native project artifacts that should be considered part of your Ionic app (i.e., check them into source control, edit them in their own IDEs, etc.).

Learn more about [adding platforms here](https://capacitorjs.com/docs/getting-started/with-ionic#add-platforms)
.

### Building a Native Binary using Appflow[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/android#building-a-native-binary-using-appflow "Direct link to Building a Native Binary using Appflow")

Make sure you commit your project with the latest changes to your git repository so that Appflow can access them.

note

You can build a variety of native build types on Appflow. Refer the docs to [learn more](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/build-types)
.

Trigger a web build from the most recent commit.

1.  Select **Android** as the target platform.
2.  Select **Debug** as the build type.
3.  Select the `latest` build stack (learn more about [build stacks here](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/build-stacks)
    ).
4.  Click **Build**.

![Appflow-create-android-build](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/create-android-build-be7d89147d17f9238e477c20b907d277.png)

Once the build starts, you should be able to see the logs from the build instance.

### Download the Starter App[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/android#download-the-starter-app "Direct link to Download the Starter App")

After your build has completed, you can download the `.apk` or `.aab` file then load it onto your device/simulator of choice to see how it works.

![Appflow-android-build-log](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/android-build-log-9d9f4728ee1c92578f7faab729d17ec5.png)

To download the build artifact, click on the above highlighted build artifact link and your download starts. You can also install the `.apk` file on your simulator or device with development mode on.

Contents
--------

*   [Build starter app](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/android#build-starter-app)
    
*   [Package for Native](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/android#package-for-native)
    
*   [Building a Native Binary using Appflow](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/android#building-a-native-binary-using-appflow)

# Capacitor SDK Reference - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/capacitor#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

The Live Updates API offers programmatic access to the Live Updates SDK and the most control over the user experience.

Within your Capacitor app project, install the Live Updates SDK:

    npm install @capacitor/live-updatesnpx cap sync

With the Live Updates SDK installed, add a "LiveUpdates" configuration section under `plugins` in `capacitor.config.ts` (or `capacitor.config.json`) file. Set `autoUpdateMethod` to `none` to use the SDK API:

    import { CapacitorConfig } from '@capacitor/cli';const config: CapacitorConfig = {  plugins: {    LiveUpdates: {      appId: '042a1261',      channel: 'Production',      autoUpdateMethod: 'none',      maxVersions: 3    }  }};export default config;

### Sync[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/capacitor#sync "Direct link to Sync")

*   **sync**(): `void`
*   Description: Check for and download a new live update if available.
*   Returns: `Promise<SyncResult>`. A response describing a live update if one is available.

#### SyncResult Interface[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/capacitor#syncresult-interface "Direct link to SyncResult Interface")

*   activeApplicationPathChanged: `boolean` - `true` if a new live update has just been downloaded
*   liveUpdate.appId: `string` - Appflow App Id
*   liveUpdate.channel: `string` - Live Update channel name that the update has been deployed to
*   snapshot.buildId: `string` - Appflow Build Id
*   snapshot.id: `string` - Internal Appflow Id
*   source: `string` - `download` if a new live update has just been downloaded or `cache` if a cached live update has been found instead

    const result = await LiveUpdates.sync();

### Reload[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/capacitor#reload "Direct link to Reload")

*   **reload**(): `void`
*   Description: Reload the app. A new version will be shown afterward if available.
*   Returns: `Promise<void>`. Nothing.

    import * as LiveUpdates from '@capacitor/live-updates';// Check for new live updateconst result = await LiveUpdates.sync();// If active path has changed, then a new live update was downloadedif (result.activeApplicationPathChanged) {	// Reload the app; the new version will appear afterward	await LiveUpdates.reload();}

### Get and Set Config[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/capacitor#get-and-set-config "Direct link to Get and Set Config")

*   **setConfig**(): `LiveUpdateConfig`
*   Description: Change Live Updates configuration during runtime. Only the values provided will change. Changes are persisted between app launches, **except for when a new binary is shipped.** In that case, the persisted config is wiped and the config shipped with the application is used.
*   Returns: `Promise<void>`. Nothing.

If you can set it, you can get it!

*   **getConfig**(): `void`
*   Description: Retrieve current config settings.
*   Returns: `Promise<LiveUpdateConfig>`

    let currentConfig = await LiveUpdates.getConfig();console.log(currentConfig.channel); // productionconsole.log(currentConfig.appId); // 123await LiveUpdates.setConfig({  channel: "staging",  appId: "456"});currentConfig = await LiveUpdates.getConfig();console.log(currentConfig.channel); // stagingconsole.log(currentConfig.appId); // 456

### Reset Config[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/capacitor#reset-config "Direct link to Reset Config")

*   **resetConfig**(): `void`
*   Description: Wipes the persisted config and uses the one shipped with the application.
*   Return: `Promise<void>`. Nothing.

    await LiveUpdates.resetConfig();

### Examples[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/capacitor#examples "Direct link to Examples")

For more API usage examples, see [Live Update Strategies](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/capacitor-sdk#live-update-strategies)
.

Contents
--------

*   [Sync](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/capacitor#sync)
    
*   [Reload](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/capacitor#reload)
    
*   [Get and Set Config](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/capacitor#get-and-set-config)
    
*   [Reset Config](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/capacitor#reset-config)
    
*   [Examples](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/capacitor#examples)

# Using private NPM modules - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private_npm_modules#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Get an authentication token using the npm CLI:

    $ npm token create --read-onlynpm password:┌────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┐│ token          │ 1a583a54-5515-4058-a3c4-047e5f699d27 │├────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤│ cidr_whitelist │                                      │├────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤│ readonly       │ true                                 │├────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤│ created        │ 2019-01-08T20:53:17.461Z             │└────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────┘

Configure an `NPM_TOKEN` secret in your Appflow environment using the generated token as value:

![NPM token secret](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/npm-token-secret-ab2082176615e230663142b06eee8670.png)

Check in a `.npmrc` file in the root of your project directory with the following line:

    //registry.npmjs.org/:_authToken=${NPM_TOKEN}

---

# Using Local NPM modules - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/local-npm-modules#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

You can package an NPM module or Capacitor/Cordova plugin in your repository and reference it within your application.

To do so, you must package it as a tarball and reference the file path within your `package.json`.

For example, your project may have the following structure:

    .├── custom-plugins│   └── my-custom-plugin│       ├── package.json│       └── src├── package.json└── src

From the root of your project, your module is located at `custom-plugins/my-custom-plugin`.

To package your plugin into a tarball, run `npm pack` inside the plugin's project directory.

    $ cd custom-plugins/my-custom-plugin$ npm pack

This will create `my-custom-plugin-1.0.0.tgz` inside `custom-plugins/my-custom-plugin`.

You can then reference this dependency in your package.json and utilize it in your app:

    "dependencies": {  ...  "my-custom-plugin": "file:custom-plugins/my-custom-plugin/my-custom-plugin-1.0.0.tgz",  ...}

---

# Override Node Version - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/switch_node_version#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

It is possible to override the Node version used during an Appflow build.

To switch Node versions simply add a variable named `OVERRIDE_NODE_VERSION` inside an Appflow environment. Select the environment when triggering a build.

![Use different Node](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/switch-node-version-f4de675e3bfcc796502b9dd09fba42f4.png)

#### Notes[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/switch_node_version#notes "Direct link to Notes")

The environment variable `OVERRIDE_NODE_VERSION` can only be set to an integer representing the major version such as `20` or `22` for example.

note

Always refer to the [Build Stacks page](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/build-stacks)
 for the most up-to-date values.

Even if `12.13.0` is a valid version, it is not a valid input; Future Node LTS releases will be made available when they officially enter LTS status. Only LTS releases are included in the Appflow build system.

If `OVERRIDE_NODE_VERSION` is set to an invalid or unavailable version, the Node version will be set to the build stack's default Node version.

Contents
--------

---

# Native Build Types - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/build-types#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

There are a few different types of builds you can choose for each platform (iOS, Android) that have different characteristics and requirements.

Android Build Types[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/build-types#android-build-types "Direct link to Android Build Types")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Debug[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/build-types#debug "Direct link to Debug")

Debug type Android builds do not require any signing certificates or signing certificates and can be sideloaded directly onto Android devices.

### Release[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/build-types#release "Direct link to Release")

Release type Android builds are required if you want to release the `.aab` on the Google Play Store. They require creating [android signing certificates](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#android-credentials)
 and uploading them to a `Production` type Appflow [signing certificate](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/adding-credentials#uploading-credentials)
.

### Custom[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/build-types#custom "Direct link to Custom")

You can also specify a [custom build type](https://developer.android.com/studio/build/build-variants#build-type)
. Appflow [supports Android build variants](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/native-projects#build-type)
 for certain mobile architectures.

iOS Build Types[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/build-types#ios-build-types "Direct link to iOS Build Types")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Simulator[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/build-types#simulator "Direct link to Simulator")

Building with a simulator type allows your app to run on an iOS simulator. This build type generates an `.app` file. Simulator type iOS builds do not require a signing certificate or provisioning profile.

To open, unzip the downloaded binary and drag the app file onto an open iOS simulator.

![Opening a Simulator Build](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-simulator-build-open-file-ac243781792646443134b1c9b549dd5c.png)

### Development[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/build-types#development "Direct link to Development")

Code Signing with a development type builds allows your app to run on device through Xcode. Development type iOS builds require [generating signing certificates and a provisioning profile](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#ios-certificates)
 using:

*   iOS App Development provisioning profile
*   Apple or iOS App Development signing certificate

and should be stored in a `Development` type Appflow [signing certificate](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/adding-credentials#uploading-credentials)
.

### Ad Hoc[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/build-types#ad-hoc "Direct link to Ad Hoc")

Code Signing with a Ad Hoc type builds allows you to distribute your app to devices specified in the provisioning profile, and are also needed in order to test push notifications. Ad Hoc type iOS builds require [generating signing certificates and a provisioning profile](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#ios-certificates)
 using:

*   Distribution Ad Hoc provisioning profile
*   Apple or iOS Distribution signing certificate

and should be stored in a `Production` type Appflow [signing certificate](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/adding-credentials#uploading-credentials)
.

### App Store[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/build-types#app-store "Direct link to App Store")

Code Signing with a App Store type builds allows you to distribute you app using Testflight and the App Store. App Store type iOS builds require [generating signing certificates and a provisioning profile](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#ios-certificates)
 using:

*   Distribution App Store provisioning profile
*   Apple or iOS Distribution signing certificate

and should be stored in a `Production` type Appflow [signing certificate](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/adding-credentials#uploading-credentials)
.

### Enterprise[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/build-types#enterprise "Direct link to Enterprise")

note

Enterprise iOS builds are only available on certain plans and require you to have an [Apple Developer Enterprise Account](https://developer.apple.com/programs/enterprise/)

Code Signing with Enterprise type builds allows you to distribute your app to devices outside the App Store if you are building apps for internal use and distribution. Enterprise type iOS builds require [generating signing certificates and a provisioning profile](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#ios-certificates)
 using:

*   iOS distribution provisioning profile
*   iOS production signing certificate

and should be stored in a `Production` type Appflow [signing certificate](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/adding-credentials#uploading-credentials)
.

# Native Configurations - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/native-configs#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

Native configs allow you overwrite certain configurations without having to commit changes to version control on a per build/automation basis such as:

*   overwrite the unique bundle identifier in the [configuration file](https://capacitorjs.com/docs/config)
     of a Capacitor project
*   overwrite the unique bundle identifier or [id attribute](https://cordova.apache.org/docs/en/latest/config_ref/#widget)
     in the `config.xml` of a Cordova project
*   overwrite the App Name as it will appear on the home screen
*   overwrite the [Live Update SDK variables and preferences](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/api#plugin-variables)
    

This makes it easy to build your app for multiple environments from the same version of the code. If using an [Automation](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/automation/intro)
, you can trigger multiple automations from the same branch that will produce different builds.

Common use cases are:

*   Making Staging, QA, and Production versions of you app with different Bundle IDs, App names, and Deploy Channels so that you can install all the environments on a single device and easily tell the apart
*   Leaving the [DisableDeploy](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/api#disabledeploy)
     `true` for development and automatically setting it back to `false` when building binaries for release

note

If you plan to use Native Configurations to modify Bundle IDs with your iOS builds, you will need to set up code signing certificates using those alternate Bundle IDs. See our section on [Generating Certificates](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials)
 for more information.

To create one go to the `Build > Native Configs` tab in the sidebar and click `New native config`.

![Native configs](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-native-configs-1f5dbb51954346e22c756d09f8eaf4e6.png)

---

# Deploying Live Updates - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

Now that we've [installed the Live Updates SDK](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/capacitor-sdk)
 and understand how [web builds](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/builds)
 and [channels](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels)
 work, we'll walk through the process of deploying a live update to a device.

The Live Updates feature works by using the installed Live Updates SDK in your native application to listen to a particular Live Update [channel destination](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels)
.

When a Web build is assigned to a channel destination, that update will be deployed to user devices running binaries that are configured to listen to the specified channel.

Nomenclature note

The terms "Web build", "deploy build", "live deploy" and "live update" can be used interchangeably. They all refer to an update provided to your user's device by Appflow's Live Updates feature.

Triggering a Web Build[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update#triggering-a-web-build "Direct link to Triggering a Web Build")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In order to deploy a live update, you will first need to create a [Web build](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/builds)
.

There are two ways to do this:

*   Click the `Start build` button from the `Commits` tab ![Start Web Build from Commits](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-start-web-build-commits-0e5fe353e4d4a2d53a2b669c88be13a5.png)
*   Click the `New build` button in the top right from the `Build > Builds` tab ![New Web Build](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-new-web-build-9d7b4241f295baedc13c490625e49fc4.png)

Choose the commit to deploy then select the `Web` target platform and the `Latest` build stack. If you have configured [custom environments](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/environments#custom-environments)
, you can select one from the provided dropdown list. Finally, you can also enable the `Live Update` and pick a [channel](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/channels)
 to automatically assign the build to once it completes successfully. For the purposes here, we will leave both options blank.

Once the build begins you can watch its progress and review at the logs if you encounter errors.

![Running Web Build](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/gif-start-web-build-6ed30e0febb1b09cb58dfff6b82e66d3.gif)

Web Build Versioning[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update#web-build-versioning "Direct link to Web Build Versioning")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Once the Web Build completes, it can be restricted to a specific native build number assigned in the Web Build details. A minimum and maximum equivalent build version number are specified, which designates whether the update is compatible. After doing this, new native builds that are distributed within an app store do not conflict with the current available update from Appflow.

![Web build versioning](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/web-build-versioning-941ab002e08f3c32f206a0bbf6bc9ae9.png)

For more details and a simple use case see [this article](https://ionic.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/articles/360003567694-How-to-restrict-Deploy-updates-by-native-version)
.

Assign the Build to a Channel[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update#assign-the-build-to-a-channel "Direct link to Assign the Build to a Channel")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

note

If you have trouble getting a successful build in the previous step, you can find answers to common Deploy build errors in [this section of our knowledge base](https://ionic.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/categories/360000410474-Deploy-Builds-Git-)
.

Once you have created a Channel, go back to the **Builds** section of your app. On the right side of each Web Build, click the 3 dot ellipsis, then "Deploy Live Update" menu item.

Select the Live Update channel from the dropdown then click the Deploy button. The Live Update becomes active immediately. Once a Web Build is set as active for a Channel, any native binaries that have been configured to watch the channel will receive the new update the next time the app checks in.

Receiving a Live Update on a Device[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update#receiving-a-live-update-on-a-device "Direct link to Receiving a Live Update on a Device")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

For your application to receive a live update, you'll need to run the app on a device or an emulator.

For Capacitor projects, the easiest way to do this is to use the [cap run](https://capacitorjs.com/docs/android#running-on-the-command-line)
 command to launch your local app in an emulator or a device connected to your computer.

    npx cap run [ios | android] [options]

You can also open Xcode and Android Studio to run the app, see logs, etc.

    npx cap open [ios | android]

For Cordova projects, the equivalent command is [ionic cordova run](https://ionicframework.com/docs/cli/commands/cordova-run)
.

    ionic cordova run [ios | android] [options]

If the app is configured correctly to listen on the channel that the live update was deployed to, the application should be ready to update. If you're using the [background update method](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/capacitor-sdk#background)
, just stay in the app for 30 seconds or so while the update is downloaded in the background. Then, close the application, reopen it again and you should see your update applied 🎉.

If the app is configured correctly to listen on the channel that the live update was deployed to, the application should update immediately on startup if you're using the [auto update method](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/capacitor-sdk#force-update)
.

Turning off Updates for Local Development[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update#turning-off-updates-for-local-development "Direct link to Turning off Updates for Local Development")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Your code will get overwritten if the local app codebase points to the same channel as a deployed Live Update build. To avoid this situation, you can turn off live updates for local development. Be sure to turn it back on before submitting to the app stores.

### Capacitor SDK[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update#capacitor-sdk "Direct link to Capacitor SDK")

Open the `capacitor.config.ts` file, and under the `LiveUpdates` section, set `enabled` to false:

    const config: CapacitorConfig = {  plugins: {    LiveUpdates: {      enabled: false,    }  }};

### Cordova SDK[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update#cordova-sdk "Direct link to Cordova SDK")

**If you have a Capacitor app**, then in the `capacitor.config.ts` file, set the following then run `npx cap sync`:

    "cordova": {  "preferences": {    "DisableDeploy": "true"  }}

Alternatively, you can change the Channel name to one currently not in use using `ionic live-update configure --channel-name="NAME"` ([details here](https://ionicframework.com/docs/cli/commands/deploy-configure)
). This make changes to the native project files directly (on Android, `android/app/src/main/res/xml/config.xml` and on iOS, `ios/App/App/config.xml`).

**If you have a Cordova app**, then set the [DisableDeploy](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/api#disabledeploy)
 preference to `true` in the `config.xml` file during local development. You can then set it back to `false` before building your binary for release by either manually setting it back or using the [native config feature](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/native-configs)
 if you're building your binaries with the [native builds](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/intro)
 feature.

Review Live Updates Strategies[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update#review-live-updates-strategies "Direct link to Review Live Updates Strategies")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Downloading and applying Live Updates automatically in the background is just one of several Live Updates strategies you can apply. Next, let's look at some advanced options.

Contents
--------

*   [Triggering a Web Build](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update#triggering-a-web-build)
    
*   [Web Build Versioning](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update#web-build-versioning)
    
*   [Assign the Build to a Channel](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update#assign-the-build-to-a-channel)
    
*   [Receiving a Live Update on a Device](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update#receiving-a-live-update-on-a-device)
    
*   [Turning off Updates for Local Development](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update#turning-off-updates-for-local-development)
    *   [Capacitor SDK](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update#capacitor-sdk)
        
    *   [Cordova SDK](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update#cordova-sdk)
        
*   [Review Live Updates Strategies](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/deploy-live-update#review-live-updates-strategies)

# Using Private Git Submodules in Appflow - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/submodules#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

During the development process, it might be required to clone and use other libraries as git Submodules. Using Submodules will allow any user to clone other libraries inside any project that uses it, make the needed modifications and build using those libraries

You can use private git submodules in appflow by providing the relative path of the submodule repository from the project you would like to add the submodule to. Appflow will automatically initialize the submodule during build without any additional configuration.

note

If you are looking to clone additional private repositories during the build, you can refer to our previous guide on Using private Git repositories in Appflow which is different from adding Git submodules.

Adding the Submodule from the root of the project with a relative path would be similar to:

    git submodule add ../subrepo.git

At this make sure you commit and trigger a build Appflow initializes, clones, and checks out the submodule to be used in the build as seen below:

    Updating/initializing submodules...Submodule 'sub-repo' (https://github.com/Ionic/subrepo.git) registered for path 'subrepo'Cloning into '/builds/Ionic/repo/subrepo'...Submodule path 'subrepo': checked out 'a10a10a10'

---

# Cordova SDK Setup - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

In order to use Appflow's Live Updates feature you'll need to install and configure the Live Updates SDK.

The Cordova Live Updates SDK, `cordova-plugin-ionic`, works with both Cordova and Capacitor apps. Select this version if you have a Cordova app and are unable to [migrate to Capacitor](https://capacitorjs.com/docs/cordova/migrating-from-cordova-to-capacitor)
.

Installation[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#installation "Direct link to Installation")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

To install the SDK, run the following command in the root directory of your Ionic app, making sure to substitute the correct values for your app:

    ionic live-update add  \    --app-id="YOUR_APP_ID" \    --channel-name="YOUR_CHANNEL_NAME" \    --update-method="background|auto|none" \

You can also run `ionic live-update add` and the CLI will prompt you for the necessary values.

note

`ionic live-update` was originally `ionic deploy`. It was renamed in Ionic CLI v7.

SDK Configuration[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#sdk-configuration "Direct link to SDK Configuration")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Live Updates SDK uses variables to configure the way the SDK behaves. You can set these values when you add the SDK using flags (if using the `ionic live-update add` command) or using cordova variables (if using `ionic cordova plugin add`).

### App ID[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#app-id "Direct link to App ID")

*   **Required**
*   The app id is required to receive updates for an app in the Appflow dashboard. You can find the id on the app overview page.
*   `ionic live-update add --app-id=abcdef12`
*   `ionic cordova plugin add cordova-plugin-ionic --variable APP_ID=abcdef12`

![Find the App Id on the App Overview page](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/appflow-app-id-1ef185c63311ad403e24dc157457c214.png)

### Channel Name[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#channel-name "Direct link to Channel Name")

*   **Required**
*   The channel name is required to receive updates for an app in the Appflow dashboard and indicates the channel from which the device will receive updates. Note this can also be updated programmatically at runtime for advanced use cases. Make sure to use the _exact_ name of your channel, including the exact casing!
*   `ionic live-update add --channel-name=Production`
*   `ionic cordova plugin add cordova-plugin-ionic --variable CHANNEL_NAME=Production`

### Update Method[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#update-method "Direct link to Update Method")

*   **Default** `background`
*   The update method determines how the app will check for and apply live updates from Appflow. Possible values are:
    *   `background` (Recommended) - The app will check for updates in the background and not prolong the amount of time the splash screen is shown. If an update is available it will be downloaded and installed while the user is using the older version. The next time they launch the app or the app has been in background for the duration specified my `min-background-duration` the new version will be loaded.
    *   `auto` - The app will delay the launch of the app by extending how long the splash screen is shown while downloading any available updates. Once the update is available, the new version will be immediately shown and the splash screen will be hidden. We generally don't recommend this mode since it can lead to the splash screen showing for a long time particularly if the user is on a poor network connection.
    *   `none` - The SDK will not check for or apply updates on its own. This option offers the most control over the user experience since all update logic is applied programmatically using the [Live Updates API](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/api)
        .
*   `ionic live-update add --update-method=background`
*   `ionic cordova plugin add cordova-plugin-ionic --variable UPDATE_METHOD=background`

Please [see below](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#live-update-strategies)
 for more details on applying Live Updates.

### Max Versions[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#max-versions "Direct link to Max Versions")

*   **Default** `2`
*   This tells the SDK the number of previous updates it should keep on the device in order to speed up the rollback process if ever needed.
*   `ionic live-update add --max-versions=2`
*   `ionic cordova plugin add cordova-plugin-ionic --variable MAX_STORE=2`

### Min Background Duration[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#min-background-duration "Direct link to Min Background Duration")

*   **Default** `30`
*   This tells the SDK the number of seconds the app needs to be in the background for it to have been considered "closed." If the app has been in the background for at least this duration the SDK will check for and apply updates according to the update method as if the app were opened from a fully closed state. This is helpful for triggering updates even when a user never fully closes the app but also allowing them to page over to another app or password manager for short periods of time without triggering an update.
*   `ionic live-update add --min-background-duration=60`
*   `ionic cordova plugin add cordova-plugin-ionic --variable MIN_BACKGROUND_DURATION=60`

Live Update Strategies[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#live-update-strategies "Direct link to Live Update Strategies")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

There are several Live Updates strategies you can apply that affect how users receive Live Updates.

### Background[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#background "Direct link to Background")

In background mode (the default), the app will check for updates in the background and not prolong the amount of time the splash screen is shown. If an update is available, it will be downloaded and installed while the user is using the older version. The next time they launch the app or the app has been in background for the duration specified by `min-background-duration`, the new version is loaded.

To use background mode, set `update-method` to `background`:

    ionic live-update configure [platform] --update-method=backgroundorionic cordova plugin add cordova-plugin-ionic --variable UPDATE_METHOD=background

### Force Update[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#force-update "Direct link to Force Update")

The app will delay the launch of the app by extending how long the splash screen is shown while downloading any available updates. Once the update is available, the new version will be immediately shown and the splash screen will be hidden. We generally don't recommend this mode since it can lead to the splash screen showing for a long time particularly if the user is on a poor network connection.

To use the force update mode, set `update-method` to `auto`:

    ionic live-update configure [platform] --update-method=autoorionic cordova plugin add cordova-plugin-ionic --variable UPDATE_METHOD=auto

### API[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#api "Direct link to API")

You can use the Live Updates SDK API to directly control when live updates are downloaded and when they are applied. See the [Live Updates API page](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/api)
.

To use the API, set `update-method` to `none`:

    ionic live-update configure [platform] --update-method=noneorionic cordova plugin add cordova-plugin-ionic --variable UPDATE_METHOD=none

Build then Deploy a Live Update[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#build-then-deploy-a-live-update "Direct link to Build then Deploy a Live Update")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

With the Live Updates SDK installed and configured, it's time to learn how Live Update web builds and channels work.

Contents
--------

*   [Installation](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#installation)
    
*   [SDK Configuration](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#sdk-configuration)
    *   [App ID](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#app-id)
        
    *   [Channel Name](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#channel-name)
        
    *   [Update Method](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#update-method)
        
    *   [Max Versions](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#max-versions)
        
    *   [Min Background Duration](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#min-background-duration)
        
*   [Live Update Strategies](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#live-update-strategies)
    *   [Background](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#background)
        
    *   [Force Update](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#force-update)
        
    *   [API](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#api)
        
*   [Build then Deploy a Live Update](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/cordova-sdk#build-then-deploy-a-live-update)

# Migrating from Cordova to Capacitor-based SDK - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/migration#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

Regardless of the product you use with Live Updates, it’s easy to migrate to Capacitor-based Live Updates SDK and/or native libraries. The Capacitor-based Live Updates SDK relies on a new manifest differentials file, `live-update-manifest.json`. For reference, the Cordova SDK leverages a `pro-manifest.json` manifest file. Appflow now builds both manifest files in order to provide a seamless transition to your end users.

Cordova or Capacitor app[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/migration#cordova-or-capacitor-app "Direct link to Cordova or Capacitor app")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Perhaps unsurprisingly, the new Capacitor-based Live Updates SDK, `@capacitor/live-updates`, requires Capacitor. First, remove the Cordova `cordova-plugin-ionic` from your project.

    npm uninstall cordova-plugin-ionic

If you're working in a Capacitor project already, remove any Cordova Live Updates-related preferences from `capacitor.config.ts`:

    "cordova": {  "preferences": {    "DisableDeploy": "true"  }}

Finally, sync the project to remove the SDK and configuration from the native projects.

    npx cap sync

Next, remove the following Cordova SDK configuration variables from the iOS project in `ios/App/App/Info.plist`:

    <key>IonAppId</key><string>YOUR_APP_ID</string><key>IonChannelName</key><string>CHANNEL_NAME</string><key>IonUpdateMethod</key><string>background</string><key>IonMaxVersions</key><string>2</string><key>IonMinBackgroundDuration</key><string>30</string><key>IonApi</key><string>https://api.ionicjs.com</string>

For reference, the Capacitor SDK writes configuration values here: `ios/App/App/capacitor.config.json`.

Next, remove the following Cordova SDK configuration variables from the Android project in `android/app/src/main/res/values/strings.xml`:

    <string name="ionic_app_id">YOUR_APP_ID</string><string name="ionic_channel_name">CHANNEL_NAME</string><string name="ionic_update_method">background</string><string name="ionic_max_versions">2</string><string name="ionic_min_background_duration">30</string><string name="ionic_update_api">https://api.ionicjs.com</string>

For reference, the Capacitor SDK writes configuration values here: `android/app/src/main/assets/capacitor.config.json`.

With the Cordova SDK removed, [migrate to Capacitor](https://capacitorjs.com/docs/cordova/migrating-from-cordova-to-capacitor)
. Next, install and configure the Capacitor SDK [see install details](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/capacitor-sdk)
. Finally, a new app store binary release is required because of the SDK change. Ensure the latest web code/build is included in the native binary. Enjoy!

Since Appflow supports and ships both manifest files in a Live Update, your users won’t notice or be disrupted during the transition. If they are using a native binary with the Cordova SDK installed, the `pro-manifest.json` file will be used. If they update the native app and now have the Capacitor SDK installed, it will use the `live-update-manifest.json` file instead when applying a new live update.

### SDK Configuration Differences[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/migration#sdk-configuration-differences "Direct link to SDK Configuration Differences")

| Option | cordova-plugin-ionic | @capacitor/live-updates | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Config file | config.xml | capacitor.config.ts | SDK configuration file. |
| Live Updates manifest file | pro-manifest.json | live-update-manifest.json | See [Manifest file details](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/differentials)<br>. |
| App Id | app-id | appId | Id representing an Appflow App. |
| Channel | channel-name | channel | The channel from which the device will receive updates. |
| Max Versions | max-versions | maxVersions | Number of previous updates to cache on user's device. |
| Update Method/Strategy | background, auto, none | background, none | More options are available. [View Live Updates strategies](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/capacitor-sdk#live-update-strategies)<br>. |
| Strategy algorithm | n/a | strategy (zip, differential) | Whether the live update is a zip file or uses differentials. |
| Code Signing | n/a | key | The public key used for code signing in self-hosted live updates. |
| Min Background Duration | min-background-duration | n/a | The number of seconds the app needs to be in the background for it to have been considered "closed." |
| Turn off live updates for development | DisableDeploy (true) | enabled (false) | Your code will get overwritten if the local app codebase points to the same channel as a deployed Live Update build. |

Self-hosted Live Updates[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/migration#self-hosted-live-updates "Direct link to Self-hosted Live Updates")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Update to latest version of capacitor/live-updates: `npm i @capacitor/live-updates@latest`
*   Ensure you’re on the latest version of the Appflow CLI that supports differentials: **v0.14.5** and up
*   Follow the updated guide to setting up SHLU, this part: [https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/self-hosted#self-hosted](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/self-hosted#self-hosted)
    

Contents
--------

*   [Cordova or Capacitor app](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/migration#cordova-or-capacitor-app)
    *   [SDK Configuration Differences](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/migration#sdk-configuration-differences)
        
*   [Self-hosted Live Updates](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/migration#self-hosted-live-updates)

---

# Get your UDID - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/ios-udid#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Summary[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/ios-udid#summary "Direct link to Summary")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The UDID (Unique Device Identifier) of an iOS device identifies it to Apple. You'll need to [know the UDID](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/xcode/distributing-your-app-to-registered-devices#Collect-device-identifiers-iOS-iPadOS-tvOS-watchOS)
 of your development device in order to register the device in the Apple Developer Center.

Get UDID in Finder[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/ios-udid#get-udid-in-finder "Direct link to Get UDID in Finder")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Connect your device to your Mac machine and select 'Trust' on the device if needed. Open 'Finder' and select your device in the left sidebar.

Once the device is selected, click the label under the device name to show the serial number and UDID. Right-click to copy the UDID.

![Finder Copy UDID](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/finder-copy-udid-a426a73694f5f3009337d432cdc96693.png)

Get UDID in Xcode[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/ios-udid#get-udid-in-xcode "Direct link to Get UDID in Xcode")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Connect your device to your Mac machine and select 'Trust' on the device if needed.

In Xcode, select Window > Devices and Simulators. The popup will show the connected device. the UDID is the 'Identifier' in the device details.

![Xcode get UDID](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/xcode-get-udid-c8a00e4ffa2f9bf7011ea9c44e81a56c.png)

Contents
--------

*   [Summary](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/ios-udid#summary)
    
*   [Get UDID in Finder](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/ios-udid#get-udid-in-finder)
    
*   [Get UDID in Xcode](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/ios-udid#get-udid-in-xcode)

Now that you have completed the quickstart guide, you should now have a basic understanding of the key concepts of Appflow! The key concepts you have learned in this guide are:

     to Appflow

     to Appflow
*   [Building a native binary](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/package)
     from a commit

     for native binaries
*   Customizing the build process with [environments](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/environment)
     and [native configs](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/native-config)

*   [Deploying Live Updates](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/deploy#creating-a-web-build)
     to your app using Appflow

But you can still learn more about Appflow by exploring the main docs in the sidebar or by selecting a card below! For example, enterprise users will find the [Appflow CLI](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/overview)
