# NPM Caching - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/caching#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Appflow has the opt-in ability to cache NPM dependencies for builds. Appflow will cache all `node_modules/` directories found in the application's file tree after your application has been built successfully.

Zipping and uploading the cache takes a short period of time so the first build with cache enabled will take longer than previous builds, but subsequent builds will take less time when utilizing the cached dependencies.

Enabling Caching[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/caching#enabling-caching "Direct link to Enabling Caching")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can either enable caching for every build for an app, or on a per-build basis.

To enable or disable caching for every build, go to the Builds settings of the app and toggle caching: ![Cache App Settings](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/cache-app-settings-13b64b42cbc9a2ce3f64a0a370c9a8de.png)

To enable or disable caching on a per-build basis, toggle caching on the new build screen: ![Cache Per Build](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/cache-per-build-9bd841a48d5c8efd74023b85c4503c14.png)

Manage your Caches[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/caching#manage-your-caches "Direct link to Manage your Caches")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can also view and delete your caches from the Caching section of the app's Builds settings: ![Cache List](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/cache-list-7190e9d9d6e1c0029cc6b0e3e8962760.png)

CLI[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/caching#cli "Direct link to CLI")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Currently, caching cannot be enabled on a per-build basis via the Appflow CLI. However, if caching is enabled in the app's settings then Appflow CLI builds will take advantage of the cache.

Custom Dependency Install Command[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/caching#custom-dependency-install-command "Direct link to Custom Dependency Install Command")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you are overriding the default dependency install command via the [Appflow Config](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config)
 with `npm ci` then NPM will not utilize the cache.

Contents
--------

*   [Enabling Caching](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/caching#enabling-caching)
    
*   [Manage your Caches](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/caching#manage-your-caches)
    
*   [CLI](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/caching#cli)
    
*   [Custom Dependency Install Command](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/caching#custom-dependency-install-command)

---

# Native Configs - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/native-configs#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Native configs allow you to overwrite certain configurations without having to commit changes to version control on a per build/automation basis.

A few common use cases for native configurations include:

*   Overwrite the unique bundle ID or App ID in [Capacitor configuration](https://capacitorjs.com/docs/basics/configuring-your-app#native-configuration)
    .
*   Overwrite the App Name as it will appear on the home screen.
*   Overwrite the [Cordova Live Update SDK variables and preferences](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/api#plugin-variables)
    

Create a QA version of your app[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/native-configs#create-a-qa-version-of-your-app "Direct link to Create a QA version of your app")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

With Native Configs, you can make Staging, QA, and Production versions of your app, all installable on the same device and with the ability to easily tell them apart. This makes it easy to build your app for multiple environments from the same version of the code.

1.  To create a native configuration, go to the `Build > Native Configs` tab in the sidebar and click `New native config`.

![Appflow-new-native-config](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/new-native-config-0d7be525411896dd41f0bdd9cf2181ba.png)

2.  For the QA version of your app, we need a different bundle ID and app name so that you can install it on a single device and easily distinguish from the development version.

![Appflow-native-config](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/native-config-29e8334d7570b28935667f0050625d3f.png)

3.  Now you can use this native configuration to apply these values at build time. Simply select the QA native configuration when preparing a new build to have Appflow apply the appropriate values.

![Appflow-automation-native-config](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/selected-native-config-77f23fc3a506c9a51016dd8a0062c6a1.png)

**Note:** If you plan to use Native Configurations to modify Bundle IDs with your iOS builds, you will need to set up code signing credentials using those alternate Bundle IDs. See our section on [Generating Credentials](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials)
 for more information.

Contents
--------

*   [Create a QA version of your app](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/native-configs#create-a-qa-version-of-your-app)

---

# Web Build and Web Previews - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/web-preview#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

Next up, we'll explore web builds and web previews, which allow you to quickly share the web version of your app with your coworkers, stakeholders, and more.

note

Check out [this branch](https://github.com/ionic-team/tutorial-appflow-demo/tree/1_web_builds)
 of this tutorial's app repository to compare your starter project and make changes if necessary.

### Creating a web build[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/web-preview#creating-a-web-build "Direct link to Creating a web build")

Now that you have created a new App and have commits visible in your Appflow Dashboard, you can prepare a web build then view it as a web preview. There are two ways to create a web build:

*   Click the **Start build** button from the **Commits** tab. ![Appflow-build-from-commits](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/build-from-commit-0e6a11e71308a85a57329aae623b17d7.png)
*   Click the **New build** button in the top right from the **Build > Builds** tab. ![Appflow-build-new-build](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-new-web-build-9d7b4241f295baedc13c490625e49fc4.png)

Select the initial commit and then select "Web" as the target platform. Toggle the "Web preview" option so that it's enabled. Next, select the `latest` build stack. ![Appflow-trigger-web-build](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/trigger-web-build-811f0466d85f3936c8363ea54fe27bee.png)

Once you click the **Build** button, the web build will begin in a new container. You can view the build logs in realtime as your app is being built. This process should take 3-6 minutes. Once the build is successful, you can click the **Preview** button to view the preview of your starter application.

![Appflow-build-success-preview](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/build-success-preview-395bbdf2b8669b070768ab4d32ef967e.png)

Now, you can see the web preview of your starter project.

![Appflow-starter-web-preview](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/starter-web-preview-50706ba7aac498ebfaedea76e31a45d4.png)

**Notable Web Preview Features**

*   You can share your web preview with ease using the **Share** button at the top right. This can help expedite the development and design review process.
    
*   Scan the QR code (bottom right) with any Android/iPhone camera to view the same web preview in your device browser.
    

To learn about web previews, [see here](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/previews/web-previews)
.

Contents
--------

*   [Creating a web build](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/tutorial/web-preview#creating-a-web-build)

---

# Appflow Config Customizations & Monorepo Support - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

The `appflow.config.json` allows you to specify specific behaviors for:

*   App directory - in what directory should Appflow start building your app
*   Dependency installation - what commands should Appflow use to install dependencies
*   Web build - what commands should Appflow call to generate the web build
*   Native project paths - in what directory your native project files are located within your repository

Appflow integrates with npm for dependency installation and to generate the web portion of the build, though for some scenarios such as monorepos, yarn, lerna, and other specific cases different integrations might be needed.

In addition, Appflow assumes that there is one app in each Git repo and that app is in the repo's root, which, especially for monorepos, may not be true.

Overview[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config#overview "Direct link to Overview")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The `appflow.config.json` should be placed in the root of the Git repo and there should only ever be one.

The format of the config file is as such:

    {    "apps":    [        {            "appId": "a0000a0a",            "root": "apps/my-app",            "dependencyInstallCommand": "<install_cmd>",            "webBuildCommand": "<web_build_cmd>",            "iosPath": "<ios_path>",            "androidPath": "<android_path>",        },        ...    ]}

*   `apps` - the apps block can contain one or more app block definitions.

Each app block can contain the following elements. The only required element of the app block is `appId`. Unused elements out can be left out.

*   `appId` - the id of the Application in Appflow, this can be seen on the Application overview page. This is the only required element of the app block
*   `root` - the directory that Appflow should build the Application from, all commands including `dependencyInstallCommand` and `webBuildCommand` are called from this location. Left unset Appflow defaults to the root of the Git repo.
*   `dependencyInstallCommand` - the command to call to install Application dependencies. It can include navigation to a different directory, if required. `npm install` will be called if it is not set.
*   `webBuildCommand` - the command to call to generate the web build for the Application. It can include navigation to a different directory, if required. `npm run build` (or `npm run appflow:build`, see [here](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-build)
     for details on `appflow:build`) will be called if it is not set.
*   `iosPath`/`androidPath` - the path to the directory where native project files (.xcproject, .xcworkspace for iOS apps, top-level build.gradle for Android apps) are located within the repo. If unspecified, Appflow will assume the project files are in `ios` or `android` directories directly under the root. (**Note:** if `root` is specified, `iosPath` and `androidPath` are interpreted as relative to `root`)

note

For `dependencyInstallCommand` and `webBuildCommand` the provided command is called as a shell command, so if needed the current directory can be changed by a command formatted like this:

    ..."dependencyInstallCommand": "cd ../.. && yarn install",...

Specific Examples[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config#specific-examples "Direct link to Specific Examples")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Simple folders[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config#simple-folders "Direct link to Simple folders")

A Git repo with two apps each in their own subfolder and no other requirements.

    ├── /│   ├── apps/│       ├── my-app1/│       └── my-app2/

    {    "apps":    [        {            "appId": "a1111a1a",            "root": "apps/my-app1"        },        {            "appId": "b2222b2b",            "root": "apps/my-app2"        }    ]}

### Yarn[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config#yarn "Direct link to Yarn")

A Git repo with two apps each in their own subfolder and with Yarn used for installing dependencies.

    ├── /│   ├── apps/│       ├── my-app1/│       └── my-app2/

    {    "apps":    [        {            "appId": "a1111a1a",            "root": "apps/my-app1",            "dependencyInstallCommand": "cd ../.. && yarn install"        },        {            "appId": "b2222b2b",            "root": "apps/my-app2",            "dependencyInstallCommand": "cd ../.. && yarn install"        }    ]}

### Lerna[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config#lerna "Direct link to Lerna")

A Git repo with two apps each in their own subfolder and with Lerna used for installing dependencies.

    ├── /│   ├── apps/│       ├── my-app1/│       └── my-app2/

    {    "apps":    [        {            "appId": "a1111a1a",            "root": "apps/my-app1",            "dependencyInstallCommand": "cd ../../ && npx lerna bootstrap"        },        {            "appId": "b2222b2b",            "root": "apps/my-app2",            "dependencyInstallCommand": "cd ../../ && npx lerna bootstrap"        }    ]}

### Nx[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config#nx "Direct link to Nx")

A Git repo with two apps in their own subfolder and with Nx used for installing dependencies and building.

note

We currently recommend using the [nxtend](https://nxtend.dev/docs/nxtend/introduction)
 Ionic community plugins for developing Ionic applications in an Nx workspace. However, these plugins are third-party and are not officially supported by Ionic.

    ├── /│   ├── apps/│       ├── my-app1/│       └── my-app2/

    {    "apps":    [        {            "appId": "a1111a1a",            "root": "apps/my-app1",            "dependencyInstallCommand": "yarn install && cd ../../ && yarn install",            "webBuildCommand": "cd ../../ && yarn nx build my-app1"        },        {            "appId": "b2222b2b",            "root": "apps/my-app2",            "dependencyInstallCommand": "yarn install && cd ../../ && yarn install",            "webBuildCommand": "cd ../../ && yarn nx build my-app2"        }    ]}

### Custom build commands[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config#custom-build-commands "Direct link to Custom build commands")

A Git repo with a single app requiring a special web build command for two different types of builds, note each build here needs to be a different app in Appflow

    ├── /

    {    "apps":    [        {            "appId": "a1111a1a",            "webBuildCommand": "npm run mybuild"        },        {            "appId": "b2222b2b",            "webBuildCommand": "npm run otherbuild"        }    ]}

### Custom native project paths[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config#custom-native-project-paths "Direct link to Custom native project paths")

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

A git repo containing native iOS and Android apps in subdirectories.

    ├── /│   ├── iMyApp/│   │   ├── MyApp.xcodeproj/│   │   └── MyApp.xcworkspace/│   └── MyAppDroid/│       ├── build.gradle│       └── app/

    {    "apps":    [        {            "appId": "a1111a1a",            "iosPath": "iMyApp"        },        {            "appId": "b2222b2b",            "androidPath": "MyAppDroid"        }    ]}

Contents
--------

*   [Overview](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config#overview)
    
*   [Specific Examples](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config#specific-examples)
    *   [Simple folders](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config#simple-folders)
        
    *   [Yarn](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config#yarn)
        
    *   [Lerna](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config#lerna)
        
    *   [Nx](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config#nx)
        
    *   [Custom build commands](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config#custom-build-commands)
        
    *   [Custom native project paths](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/appflow-config#custom-native-project-paths)

# SDK Changelog - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

The Live Update SDK is used with Appflow's Live Update feature to detect and sync your app with new updates that you've pushed to channels.

The original live update SDK, `cordova-plugin-ionic`, works with Cordova and Capacitor apps.

The next generation live update SDK, `@capacitor/live-updates`, supports [Capacitor apps](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/capacitor-sdk)
 and [self-hosted live updates](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/self-hosted)
 for enterprise customers.

@capacitor/live-updates[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#capacitorlive-updates "Direct link to @capacitor/live-updates")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

### 0.4.0[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#040 "Direct link to 0.4.0")

*   Upgraded plugin to support Capacitor 7.

### 0.3.1[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#031 "Direct link to 0.3.1")

*   Fixed an issue where progress callback was not being properly executed in certain cases.

### 0.3.0[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#030 "Direct link to 0.3.0")

*   Added a progress callback to the `sync` function, allowing download progress to be tracked.

### 0.2.1[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#021 "Direct link to 0.2.1")

*   Fixed compilation problem.

### 0.2.0[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#020 "Direct link to 0.2.0")

*   Upgraded plugin to support Capacitor 6.

### 0.1.3[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#013 "Direct link to 0.1.3")

*   Added support for custom URL tokens.

### 0.1.2[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#012 "Direct link to 0.1.2")

*   Fixed an iOS issue where the plugin was still checking for the presence of a public key even when disabled.

### 0.1.1[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#011 "Direct link to 0.1.1")

*   Released plugin for general availability.

### 0.1.1-beta.6[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#011-beta6 "Direct link to 0.1.1-beta.6")

*   Added `setConfig` method to change Live Updates configuration on demand, such as the channel name. Use `getConfig` to retrieve current config settings.
*   Added `resetConfig` method that wipes the persisted config and uses the config shipped with the application.
*   Documented the ["Dynamic Configuration"](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/capacitor-sdk#dynamic-configuration)
     Update Strategy.

### 0.1.1-beta.5[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#011-beta5 "Direct link to 0.1.1-beta.5")

*   Added more descriptive error messages when there are problems communicating with a live update server.

### 0.1.1-beta.4[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#011-beta4 "Direct link to 0.1.1-beta.4")

*   Fixed an Android issue where disabling the Live Updates plugin didn't work.
*   Added missing "enabled" flag to TypeScript definition of the plugin
*   Known issue: 'enabled' does not exist in type LiveUpdateConfig \*\* This will be resolved in Capacitor v6. For now, ignore this or add `// @ts-ignore` above the field.

### 0.1.1-beta.3[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#011-beta3 "Direct link to 0.1.1-beta.3")

*   Fixed an Android issue with file copying on device.
*   First beta launch of the Capacitor Live Updates SDK.

### 0.1.1-beta.2[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#011-beta2 "Direct link to 0.1.1-beta.2")

*   Add the `enabled` configuration item for turning off live updates for local development.

### 0.1.1-beta.1[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#011-beta1 "Direct link to 0.1.1-beta.1")

*   Differential live update support for [Self-hosted live updates](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/setup/self-hosted)
     released for early access.
*   Capacitor Live Updates now requires Capacitor 5+ (originally v3+).
*   [Self-hosted Live Updates with Azure DevOps guide](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cli/examples/self-hosted-live-updates/azure-devops)
     updated to use differential live updates.

### 0.0.1[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#001 "Direct link to 0.0.1")

*   Initial release to support Self-hosted live updates.

cordova-plugin-ionic[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#cordova-plugin-ionic "Direct link to cordova-plugin-ionic")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

### 5.5.3[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#553 "Direct link to 5.5.3")

*   Implemented a better fix for iOS "no space left on device" error when an unusually large number of files are downloaded.

### 5.5.2[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#552 "Direct link to 5.5.2")

*   Fixed iOS "no space left on device" error that occurred when an unusually large number of files are downloaded.

### 5.5.1[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#551 "Direct link to 5.5.1")

*   Updated iOS live update logic to properly handle non-200 status code responses from the update server.

### 5.5.0[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#550 "Direct link to 5.5.0")

*   Fixed situation where a live update for a previous binary version could be applied resulting in a stale app state
*   Removed cordova-plugin-whitelist dependency to improve cordova-android v10 compatibility
*   Fixed a deprecation with defining Android dependencies

### 5.4.8[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#548 "Direct link to 5.4.8")

*   Fixed background update mode to handle a scenario in which a live update for an older binary is applied to a new binary.

### 5.4.7[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#547 "Direct link to 5.4.7")

*   Angular Ivy support

### 5.4.6[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#546 "Direct link to 5.4.6")

*   iOS fix to create intermediate directories for downloads.

### 5.4.5[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#545 "Direct link to 5.4.5")

*   Fix Android issue to ensure parent folders exist on file creation.

### 5.4.4[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#544 "Direct link to 5.4.4")

*   Fix issue where too many network requests at once could fire and cause performance issues.

### 5.4.3[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#543 "Direct link to 5.4.3")

*   Fix issue where types caused incompatibility with Ionic v3 (Typescript 2.x)

### 5.4.0[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#540 "Direct link to 5.4.0")

*   Added Deploy API Imports
*   Removed dependency on `cordova-plugin-file`

### 5.3.0[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#530 "Direct link to 5.3.0")

*   Added an 'incompatibleUpdateAvailable' property to the 'CheckForUpdateResponse' (\[#204\] ([https://github.com/ionic-team/cordova-plugin-ionic/pull/204](https://github.com/ionic-team/cordova-plugin-ionic/pull/204)
    ))
*   'ConfigurationInfo' now contains the 'BuildId' in addition to the 'SnapshotId' (\[#204\] ([https://github.com/ionic-team/cordova-plugin-ionic/pull/204](https://github.com/ionic-team/cordova-plugin-ionic/pull/204)
    ))

### 5.2.9[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#529 "Direct link to 5.2.9")

*   Get dataDirectory from getAppInfo function ([#197](https://github.com/ionic-team/cordova-plugin-ionic/pull/197)
    )
*   Add proxy for browser platform to support it ([#199](https://github.com/ionic-team/cordova-plugin-ionic/pull/199)
    )

### 5.2.8[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#528 "Direct link to 5.2.8")

*   Fix Type Error in IDeployConfig ([#196](https://github.com/ionic-team/cordova-plugin-ionic/pull/196)
    )

### 5.2.7[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#527 "Direct link to 5.2.7")

*   Change hook to run before\_prepare and make it async ([#178](https://github.com/ionic-team/cordova-plugin-ionic/pull/178)
    )
*   Fixed bug where the new binary update would load an older cached version of the app ([#179)](https://github.com/ionic-team/cordova-plugin-ionic/issues/179)
    )

### 5.2.6[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#526 "Direct link to 5.2.6")

*   Check for Capacitor and switch folder ([#164](https://github.com/ionic-team/cordova-plugin-ionic/pull/164)
    )
*   Remove unused import ([#163](https://github.com/ionic-team/cordova-plugin-ionic/pull/163)
    )
*   Delay device ready until pro checks are done ([#161](https://github.com/ionic-team/cordova-plugin-ionic/pull/161)
    )

### 5.2.5[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#525 "Direct link to 5.2.5")

*   Fix bug where binaryVersionName and binaryVersionCode are not returned from getConfiguration call
*   Fix bug where downloadUpdate progress call back would go from 0 to 50 rather than 100 ([#156](https://github.com/ionic-team/cordova-plugin-ionic/pull/156%5D)
    )
*   Check if the device is online before checking for updates ([#154](https://github.com/ionic-team/cordova-plugin-ionic/pull/154)
    )

### 5.2.4[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#524 "Direct link to 5.2.4")

*   update check device resp to be accurate ([#148](https://github.com/ionic-team/cordova-plugin-ionic/pull/148)
    )

### 5.2.3[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#523 "Direct link to 5.2.3")

*   Fixed bug with AndroidManifest.xml syntax for real since our release script kept breaking it

### 5.2.2[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#522 "Direct link to 5.2.2")

*   Fixed bug with AndroidManifest.xml syntax

### 5.2.1[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#521 "Direct link to 5.2.1")

*   Add ACCESS\_NETWORK\_STATE permission to make navigator.onLine work on android

### 5.2.0[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#520 "Direct link to 5.2.0")

*   Added `DisableDeploy` Cordova preference allowing disabling of the plugin
*   Requires `cordova-plugin-ionic-webview@^2.1.4` for `DisableDeploy` support to work correctly

### 5.1.6[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#516 "Direct link to 5.1.6")

*   Fixed a bug with none update method strategy that could cause background updates upon resume of the app from background

### 5.0.6[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#506 "Direct link to 5.0.6")

*   Fixed a bug with version rebuilds that could make some initial redirects take up to 15 seconds.

### 5.0.5[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#505 "Direct link to 5.0.5")

*   Rebuild a deploy directory in the case where the binary version has changed since the update was downloaded.

### 5.0.0[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/deploy/sdk/changelog#500 "Direct link to 5.0.0")

*   Release!

Contents

# Native Builds - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/intro#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

Ionic Appflow makes it easy to build native app binaries for iOS and Android in the cloud. It's perfect for automating binary builds and for developers without Mac hardware that want to build iOS apps.

##### Helpful links[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/intro#helpful-links "Direct link to Helpful links")

[![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-quickstart-icon.png)![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-quickstart-icon.png)\
\
Build your very first Android debug build by following our quickstart guide.](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/package)
[![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-faq-icon.png)![](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/icons/guide-faq-icon.png)\
\
Find answers to common questions about building native binaries](https://ionic.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/categories/360000410494-Package)

---

# Connect Your Repo - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/connect#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

While Creating a new App[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/connect#while-creating-a-new-app "Direct link to While Creating a new App")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Once the Git host integration is complete, Appflow will create a new repo for your Git host account, construct a new app based on your choice of template and framework and then push the results to the new repo.

![Compiling new app](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/compiling-new-app-3fdd1f2a12f6df554591511ba3083504.png)

Then Appflow will direct you to the App's `Overview` page. Here you will see important information about your app such as its name, app id, and the URL to the new repo where the code for the app was pushed to.

![new-app-overview-info](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/new-app-overview-info-fb221e5281025aceed4ff3f2143d1494.png)

If you then navigate to the `Builds` page, you will notice that a web build was created for this app.

![build created for new app](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/build-created-for-new-app-0f2b0090ada93ce9fbaf3d74da038e1b.png)

This build contains a link to the commit that was used to create the build and a temporary web preview for you to demo the app.

![Web preview for new app](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/gif-web-preview-for-new-app-05c1de62644b37be12544035470f8f2c.gif)

While Importing an Existing App[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/connect#while-importing-an-existing-app "Direct link to While Importing an Existing App")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Once Appflow has integrated with your Git host, the page will update to show a list of repos found for this Git host. Selecting a repository from this list will direct you to the `Builds` page of the newly imported app.

![build-page-after-import](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/build-page-after-import-330d22f342548186ad4d4e11de91170c.png)

Navigating to the `Commits` page will reveal up to the last five commits that were pushed to the repo.

![commits-list-after-import](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/commits-list-after-import-a37d33c4fc1038362168794f795107e6.png)

Ready for Commits[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/connect#ready-for-commits "Direct link to Ready for Commits")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Now that Appflow has connected to a repo, it will automatically create a webhook on that repo. This webhook will send push events to Appflow to keep track of the commits it can use to create builds for your app. With this, you are now ready to push a commit for Appflow!

Contents
--------

*   [While Creating a new App](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/connect#while-creating-a-new-app)
    
*   [While Importing an Existing App](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/connect#while-importing-an-existing-app)
    
*   [Ready for Commits](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/connect#ready-for-commits)

# Private Ionic Native Enterprise Edition Keys in Open Source Apps - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private-native-enterprise-keys#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

If you're building a public, open source app that uses Ionic Native Enterprise Edition, you'll likely want to keep the native key secret.

There's just a few steps involved to get this working locally and in CI/CD platforms.

note

Any developer building the app locally will still need access to the native key.

About Native Plugin Keys[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private-native-enterprise-keys#about-native-plugin-keys "Direct link to About Native Plugin Keys")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

In order to install and use Ionic Native Enterprise Edition solutions and premier plugins in your app, a native plugin key is required to authenticate access. After [assigning a key](https://ionic.io/docs/premier-plugins/setup)
 to an app in Appflow, you run `ionic enterprise register` to register the native key, enabling the ability to install Enterprise solutions/plugins locally.

By default, the `register` command places the native key into `ionic.config.json` and `.npmrc` files. Normally, both of these changes should be committed to version control so that CI/CD systems as well as other team members can restore the plugins from a fresh clone of the app's repository.

However, if the app is open source, this effectively makes the native key public. To keep it private, follow these steps.

Register Native Key[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private-native-enterprise-keys#register-native-key "Direct link to Register Native Key")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Create an `.npmrc` file in the root of the app project, then add the following:

    @ionic-enterprise:registry=https://registry.ionicframework.com///registry.ionicframework.com/:_authToken=${ENT_NATIVE_KEY}

The `ENT_NATIVE_KEY` part represents the Enterprise Native Key in variable format that will be swapped in dynamically, and can be named however you'd like.

Alternatively, run `ionic enterprise register` then be sure to remove the native key from `ionic.config.json` and change the `_authToken` value to `ENT_NATIVE_KEY` or similar.

Configure Local Development Environment[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private-native-enterprise-keys#configure-local-development-environment "Direct link to Configure Local Development Environment")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

When you run `npm install` now, it will fail:

`Failed to replace env in config: ${ENT_NATIVE_KEY}`

npm can no longer authenticate with the Ionic Enterprise registry - it needs a native key.

### Mac[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private-native-enterprise-keys#mac "Direct link to Mac")

Add the native key to a `.bashrc` or `.bash_profile` file:

    export ENT_NATIVE_KEY=key_4e243d

### Windows[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private-native-enterprise-keys#windows "Direct link to Windows")

Add the native key as an Environment Variable. Navigate to `System Properties -> Advanced -> Environment Variables`. Under "User variables for \[login name\]", add a new Variable (`ENT_NATIVE_KEY`) and Value (\[the native key\]).

Re-run `npm install` to install the native plugins. You may need to close and relaunch any open terminals so the new environment variable can take effect.

Configure Appflow with a Custom Environment[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private-native-enterprise-keys#configure-appflow-with-a-custom-environment "Direct link to Configure Appflow with a Custom Environment")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

To build a native version of your app in the cloud with Appflow, configure a [Custom Environment](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/environments#custom-environments)
.

Within Appflow, open the app you wish to work on and navigate in the sidebar to `Build -> Environments`, then click `New Environment` (or select an existing environment).

Under `Secrets`, add a new entry: `ENT_NATIVE_KEY` for the Key and the native key for the Value. After saving, select the Environment that contains the Enterprise Native Key when building new native binaries or web builds.

Configuring other CI/CD Systems[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private-native-enterprise-keys#configuring-other-cicd-systems "Direct link to Configuring other CI/CD Systems")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Configuring other CI/CD systems is similar to Appflow: add a new `ENT_NATIVE_KEY` entry per environment so that the Enterprise Native key is used in each build.

For example, in **Vercel**, this is configured within a project's settings screen, then the Environment Variables page.

Contents
--------

*   [About Native Plugin Keys](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private-native-enterprise-keys#about-native-plugin-keys)
    
*   [Register Native Key](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private-native-enterprise-keys#register-native-key)
    
*   [Configure Local Development Environment](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private-native-enterprise-keys#configure-local-development-environment)
    *   [Mac](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private-native-enterprise-keys#mac)
        
    *   [Windows](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private-native-enterprise-keys#windows)
        
*   [Configure Appflow with a Custom Environment](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private-native-enterprise-keys#configure-appflow-with-a-custom-environment)
    
*   [Configuring other CI/CD Systems](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/cookbook/private-native-enterprise-keys#configuring-other-cicd-systems)

# Push a Commit - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/push#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

In most cases, connecting your repo will import the five latest commits for your app. However, if the imported commits don't include the version needed for your build, you'll need to push a commit so that it shows up in your Dashboard.

Committing to Appflow[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/push#committing-to-appflow "Direct link to Committing to Appflow")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you are using an integration with a third party Git Host such as, GitHub a new commit will show up every time you push to that Git host.

If you are using Appflow's internal Git service, you'll need to push your commit to Appflow directly to see your commit in the Dashboard.

*   Third-Party Git Host
*   Internal Git Service

After integrating Appflow with a [third-party Git host](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/integrate-git-host)
, push commits to your remote branch with the following commands:

    git add . # stage any changesgit commit -m "added live update sdk" #  commit staged changesgit push origin main # push the changes from the main branch to your git host

After connecting to [Appflow's internal Git service](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/ionic-remote)
, push commits to your remote branch with the following commands:

    git add . # stage any changesgit commit -m "added live update sdk" #  commit staged changesgit push ionic main # push the changes from the main branch to the Ionic remote

Once you've done a `git push`, you should see your commit available in the `Commits` tab of the Dashboard.

Ready to Build[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/push#ready-to-build "Direct link to Ready to Build")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Now that Appflow has detected the new commit, it can now use the commit to create a new native build!

![Commits List](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-commit-list-277cf93d09092b90babc36fa73622703.png)

Contents
--------

*   [Committing to Appflow](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/push#committing-to-appflow)
    
*   [Ready to Build](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/push#ready-to-build)

---

# Native Builds - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/builds#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

Overview[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/builds#overview "Direct link to Overview")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A native build of your application is platform specific and can run on a physical device. Native builds produce native build artifacts such as `aab` files for Android and `ipa` files for iOS.

After the native build artifacts are generated:

*   The binaries will be stored and available for you to download from the dashboard.
*   You can find the history and status of your native builds as well as download successful builds in the side menu under `Builds` in the `Build` tab.

The steps of the native build will depend on the framework used to develop the project.

### Capacitor/Cordova[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/builds#capacitorcordova "Direct link to Capacitor/Cordova")

Native Builds for both Capacitor and Cordova start with the following steps:

*   `npm install` will run to install any dependencies you have.
*   `npm run build` will run if a `build` script is detected in the `scripts` section of your `package.json` in order to build the web app components for your application.

A native build for a Capacitor project will then follow these steps:

*   `npx cap sync [ios|android]` will run with the platform you have configured. This will copy the newly generated web app components to the corresponding native platform directory and update any native plugins found in that directory.
*   For an iOS build, `gym` is executed to produce the `ipa` and `dsym` files (or `app` file for Simulator builds).
*   For an Android build, `gradle` is used to produce the `aab` and `apk` files.

While a native build for a Cordova project will then follow these steps:

*   `cordova platform add [ios|android]` will run with the platform you have configured. `NOTE:` _DO NOT_ commit your `platforms` directory as this will cause your build to fail.
*   `cordova build [ios|android]` will run with the platform and options you have configured and generate the native binaries.

### React Native[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/builds#react-native "Direct link to React Native")

Native builds for React Native all start with the following steps:

*   Detects the package manager and installs dependencies based on configuration or default for package manager (`yarn`, `npm ci`, etc.)
*   Detects if the project is using a React Native variant like Expo.
*   For Expo projects, the prebuild command is run to generate native code.

note

For Expo projects, an `ios.bundleIdentifier` for iOS and `android.package` for Android must be set in your `app.json` for a successful native build.

Then:

*   For an iOS build, `gym` is executed to produce the `ipa` and `dsym` files (or `app` file for Simulator builds).
*   For an Android build, `gradle` is used to produce the `aab` and `apk` files.

Performing a Build[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/builds#performing-a-build "Direct link to Performing a Build")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

A native build can be triggered from the **Commits** or the **Build** tab.

From your commits list, click "Start build" on the commit you'd like to use to create the native build. You can also click "New build" from the Builds list and select a commit to use.

On the new build page, choose the type of build you want to perform, as well as any signing certificates, custom environment, or native config. Once you have a successful build, you can download it by clicking the file name in the `Artifacts` section in the right of the build detail page or clicking the `Download IPA/AAB` icon on the build in the `Build > Builds` tab.

You can rerun a build from the Builds list or the log of a build. Rerunning a build uses the same stack, environment, configuration, and other settings from the initial build attempt. A new build number and build ID are generated for the rerun, as well as a new build in the Builds list.

Concurrency limits[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/builds#concurrency-limits "Direct link to Concurrency limits")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Each account plan has a defined maximum amount of concurrent builds allowed. This limit is enforced at the account level across different apps.

When you trigger a build, in the Appflow Dashboard you will notice that a build can have different statuses before it is processed:

*   **Queued**: this means that your build is queued for the next available runner. Usually your build should not be in this state for more than few seconds.
*   **Pending**: this means that you have reached your concurrent build limit and your build will continue when your current builds are completed.

After the build starts to be processed the statuses are:

*   **Running**: the build is currently being processed. You can read the logs in the build details.
*   **Successful**: the build terminated in a correct state.
*   **Failed**: the build terminated in an incorrect state.

Contents
--------

*   [Overview](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/builds#overview)
    *   [Capacitor/Cordova](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/builds#capacitorcordova)
        
    *   [React Native](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/builds#react-native)
        
*   [Performing a Build](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/builds#performing-a-build)
    
*   [Concurrency limits](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/builds#concurrency-limits)

# Add an App - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/add-an-app#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

To begin your journey with Appflow, you will first add an app to your Appflow organization.

After logging into the Appflow dashboard, you will be directed to the `Apps` page which contains the list of apps associated with your Appflow organization. When logging into the dahsboard for the first time, a welcome message will appear on this page presenting the option to either [create an app from a template](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/add-an-app#creating-an-app-from-a-template)
 or to [import an existing app](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/add-an-app#importing-an-existing-app)
.

![Apps Page Welcome Message](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/apps-page-welcome-message-159abebe2f3d22c3d055e45ea4d3f8b6.png)

If there are apps already associated with your organization, then these options can be accessed through the dropdown of the `New app` button in the top right corner of the `Apps` page.

![Add an app from dropdown](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/add-app-from-dropdown-27b9a1cf07e88296de2728e4b86c9fe0.png)

Creating an App from a Template[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/add-an-app#creating-an-app-from-a-template "Direct link to Creating an App from a Template")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you do not already have an app to import into Appflow, then you can use Appflow to automatically create an app for you!

After selecting the `Use template` button or `Create from template` dropdown on the `Apps` page, you will be prompted to enter some information about your new app.

![Create A New App](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-create-app-868c6213a630bf7bf3ddb6a61ae8d423.png)

The `Pick a template` cards describes how your new app will be structured. For example, the `tabs` template will organize your views/screens into tabs, while the `menu` template will provide a menu to navigate between the views/screens.

The `Javascript framework` cards describes the web app framework that your new app will be built upon. Supported frameworks include [Angular](https://angular.io/)
, [React](https://react.dev/)
, and [Vue](https://vuejs.org/)
.

After selecting your cards, Appflow will use this information to construct a [Capacitor](https://capacitorjs.com/docs)
 app with your corresponding selections. The Capacitor app will also use UI components from the [Ionic Framework](https://ionicframework.com/docs)
 to give your app a native look and feel.

Importing an Existing App[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/add-an-app#importing-an-existing-app "Direct link to Importing an Existing App")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you have an existing app you wish to import into Appflow then select the `Import app` button or `Import app` dropdown option on the `Apps` page. You will then be prompted to provide additional information about the app.

![Import An Existing App](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-import-app-4bec028ae4eccebd23679450793deb67.png)

The `Select your native runtime` cards describes the native layer associated with your imported app. native runtimes like [Capacitor](https://capacitorjs.com/docs)
, [Cordova](https://cordova.apache.org/)
, or [React Native](https://reactnative.dev/)
 describe apps that are partitioned into a web layer and native layer to enable cross-platform functionality. native runtimes like `iOS Native` and `Android Native` describe apps that are only compatible with their corresponding mobile platform.

The `Select your app's git host` list provides possible locations where Appflow will look for your app's source code. Select the git host associated with your app by pressing the `Connect` button next to the desired Git host option.

Completing the Create/Import App Form[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/add-an-app#completing-the-createimport-app-form "Direct link to Completing the Create/Import App Form")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

After pressing the `continue` button on the `Create from Template` form or after pressing one of the `Connect` buttons on the `Import app` form, you will be prompted to integrate with a Git host.

Contents
--------

*   [Creating an App from a Template](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/add-an-app#creating-an-app-from-a-template)
    
*   [Importing an Existing App](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/add-an-app#importing-an-existing-app)
    
*   [Completing the Create/Import App Form](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/quickstart/add-an-app#completing-the-createimport-app-form)

---

# iOS and Android Projects - Appflow

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/native-projects#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

iOS Schemes[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/native-projects#ios-schemes "Direct link to iOS Schemes")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

Appflow will attempt to automatically detect the scheme to use for iOS builds. You may choose to use custom [Xcode schemes](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/xcode/customizing-the-build-schemes-for-a-project)
.

Override automatic scheme detection by setting the `IOS_SCHEME` environment variable.

![Use Custom Scheme](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-xcode-schemes-9d4b4acfe851ace5445fb30dedba73c8.png)

note

If `IOS_SCHEME` is set to an invalid scheme, Appflow will fall back to automatic scheme detection. If there are multiple available schemes, the build will fail.

Android Variants[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/native-projects#android-variants "Direct link to Android Variants")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/#supported-mobile-architectures)

Appflow supports custom build variants for Android projects. This includes both Build Types and Flavors, which can be customized in your projects [Gradle configuration](https://developer.android.com/studio/build/build-variants)
.

Appflow will only provide artifacts for one build variation. If your configuration has multiple variations, but you do not specify a custom build type and/or flavor, Appflow will generate the last variant (build type + flavor) ordered alphabetically.

### Build Type[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/native-projects#build-type "Direct link to Build Type")

A [custom build type](https://developer.android.com/studio/build/build-variants#build-types)
 can be specified by setting the `ANDROID_BUILD_TYPE` environment variable in Appflow. If this variable is set, Appflow will ignore the [Build Type](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/build-types)
 setting.

![Use Custom Build Type](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-android-variant-build-type-020838b7679702ac51f50d955f68acf4.png)

### Product Flavor[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/native-projects#product-flavor "Direct link to Product Flavor")

A [custom product flavor](https://developer.android.com/studio/build/build-variants#product-flavors)
 can be specified by setting the `ANDROID_FLAVOR` environment variable in Appflow. If this variable is set to an invalid option, the build will fail.

![Use Custom Flavor](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-android-variant-flavor-e9be3ef19f1122b0f908d276e90d941c.png)

note

Appflow only supports a single flavor dimension. If your project's android gradle configuration contains more than one, builds will fail.

Contents
--------

*   [iOS Schemes](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/native-projects#ios-schemes)
    
*   [Android Variants](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/native-projects#android-variants)
    *   [Build Type](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/native-projects#build-type)
        
    *   [Product Flavor](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/native-projects#product-flavor)

---

# Generating App Signing Certificates (iOS & Android)

[Skip to main content](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

On this page

The Signing Certificates feature in Appflow securely stores your credentials so you can easily reference them when building your app in the cloud.

Overview[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#overview "Direct link to Overview")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The type of signing credentials will vary based on platform (iOS and Android) as well as the type of build, such as for production or development. These credentials are not generated in Appflow. Use the following instructions to generate the correct files to upload to the Appflow dashboard for native builds.

Android Certificates[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#android-certificates "Direct link to Android Certificates")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Signing your app for release to Google Play Store involves two keys:

*   Upload key used during the build process to generate a signed bundle
*   App signing key used by the Google Play Store before deploying your app

Appflow uses the **upload key** to generate signed app bundles for release during the cloud build process. The upload key is stored in an [Android keystore](https://developer.android.com/training/articles/keystore.html)
, which is a `.keystore` or `.jks` file.

note

If you have previously released your app in the Google Play Store, you may already have an upload key on your machine. Do not create a new upload key for Appflow, use the existing key associated with your app.

### Generating in Android Studio[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#generating-in-android-studio "Direct link to Generating in Android Studio")

In Android Studio, navigate to Build > Generated Signed Bundle or APK.

![Android Studio Generate Signed Bundle or APK](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/android-studio-generate-signed-727bbe5722ee949f19a5a8ea0b81b02e.png)

Click 'Next', then on the second screen, click 'Create New' under the keystore path.

![Creat New Key](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/android-studio-new-key-e6574acfe2c712d674f7aab9b2c675cb.png)

In the popup, select the path to store your keystore file, and set a keystore password, key alias, key password, the key validity time, and your information for the Certificate. Click 'Ok' to generate the new keystore file in the designated path.

![Creat New Key Store](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/android-studio-new-key-store-9718d275b8917a0c2dabd659d24b31d7.png)

danger

Securely store a backup of your keystore password, key password, alias and keystore file. These are not recoverable and can prevent you ever being able to update an App in the Play Store.

### Generating with `keytool`[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#generating-with-keytool "Direct link to generating-with-keytool")

Keytool is a command-line tool included in the [Java JDK](http://www.oracle.com/technetwork/java/javase/downloads/jdk8-downloads-2133151.html)
.

To generate a keystore, run the command below, changing `MY-RELEASE-KEY` and `MY_ALIAS_NAME` to be relevant to your app. The tool will ask you to enter a keystore password and a key password. Take note of these values, because you will need them when adding your credentials to Appflow.

    $ keytool -genkey -v -keystore MY-RELEASE-KEY.keystore -alias MY_ALIAS_NAME -keyalg RSA -keysize 2048 -validity 10000 -storetype jks

![Generate with keytool](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/keytool-generate-aa734411754693d958ba109ef0b823eb.png)

danger

Securely store a backup of your keystore password, key password, alias and keystore file. These are not recoverable and can prevent you ever being able to update an App in the Play Store.

iOS Certificates[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#ios-certificates "Direct link to iOS Certificates")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

iOS builds in Appflow require two credentials:

*   Signing Certificate
*   Provisioning Profile

You'll need an Apple Developer account (Individual or Organization). See [comparing memberships](https://developer.apple.com/support/compare-memberships/)
.

You'll also need to register your App ID, register any devices, and optionally connect your Apple Developer account in Xcode.

### Connecting Apple Developer Account to Xcode (optional)[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#connecting-apple-developer-account-to-xcode-optional "Direct link to Connecting Apple Developer Account to Xcode (optional)")

To register devices and generate signing certificates in Xcode, you first need to connect your Apple Developer account.

In Xcode, select Xcode > Settings and then the Accounts tab. Click the + icon in the left sidebar to add your Apple ID account.

Once connected, you can select the team associated with the Apple Developer account for your Apple ID.

![Xcode connect Apple ID](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/xcode-connect-apple-id-da28ea9b3179c810e669d9b96a3dcd62.png)

### Registering your App ID[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#registering-your-app-id "Direct link to Registering your App ID")

Every app must register an ID with Apple.

*   Navigate to [Certificates, IDs & Profiles › Identifiers](https://developer.apple.com/account/resources/identifiers/list)
     in the [Apple Developer Center](https://developer.apple.com/account)
     and register a new App ID.

![iOS App id](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-profiles-ios-app-id-8c998267e4177f18ac640d61864520a4.png)

*   Under Explicit App ID, set the Bundle ID to the ID you've specified in your app's `config.xml` file. iOS Bundle IDs are represented as a reversed address, such as `com.ionicframework.MyIonicApp`.

### Device Registration[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#device-registration "Direct link to Device Registration")

Devices must be explicitly registered with Apple for Development and Ad Hoc builds.

If your Apple Developer account is connected to Xcode, you can [register devices in Xcode](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/xcode/distributing-your-app-to-registered-devices#Register-devices-automatically-in-Xcode)
 by connecting them to your machine with Xcode running and clicking the "Register" option in the popup dialog. You may need to select "Trust" on your device.

**To register a device in [Apple Developer Center](https://developer.apple.com/account)
:**

*   Navigate to [Certificates, IDs & Profiles › Devices](https://developer.apple.com/account/resources/devices/list)
     and click the + icon to register a new Device.

![iOS Register Device](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-profiles-ios-device-register-1-83dd411d65bff822d8c0c9107b53c64c.png)

*   Give your device a name and enter the device's UDID. [Find your UDID](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/ios-udid)
    .

### Signing Certificate[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#signing-certificate "Direct link to Signing Certificate")

There are two types of Apple signing certificates: Development and Distribution. Development certificates are used for Development build types, while Distribution are required for Ad Hoc, App Store, and Enterprise build types.

#### Generating Signing Certificate in Xcode (recommended)[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#generating-signing-certificate-in-xcode-recommended "Direct link to Generating Signing Certificate in Xcode (recommended)")

Generating a signing certificate in Xcode is recommended because it exports a `.p12` file without the need to generate a signing certificate request file or convert a `.cer`.

In Xcode, navigate to Xcode > Settings and then the Accounts tab. Select the team associated with your Apple Developer account and click "Manage Certificates".

In the popup, click the + icon and select the certificate type, either Development or Distribution.

![New Signing Certificate](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/xcode-new-certificate-90a3157809804600b8451f22714d4dfb.png)

Once the certificate has been generated, right click and selec "Export Certificate".

![Export Signing Certificate](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/xcode-export-cert-2f6ca5b9efea19f12edea2ee95d7bf0b.png)

Then, save the certificate, giving it a strong password. Make note of the password, as you will need it when uploading the certificate in the Appflow.

![Save Signing Certificate](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/xcode-save-cert-930669f0eb50d65bc40497497a5b724d.png)

#### Generating Certificate in Apple Developer Center[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#generating-certificate-in-apple-developer-center "Direct link to Generating Certificate in Apple Developer Center")

You can also generate a signing certificate in the Apple Developer Center. This is a multi-step process that involves:

*   Generating a `.certSigningRequest` file
*   Creating a `.cer` certificate file
*   Converting the `.cer` file into a `.p12` file

#### Generating Signing Certificate Request File[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#generating-signing-certificate-request-file "Direct link to Generating Signing Certificate Request File")

Before you can get a certificate from the Apple Developer Center, you'll need to generate a certificate signing request file. It can be created on Mac OSX by using Keychain Access and on other platforms by using OpenSSL.

**To generate using Keychain Access:**

*   Navigate to Keychain Access › Certificate Assistant › Request a Certificate From a Certificate Authority on your Mac.

![iOS CSR](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-profiles-ios-csr-52bb1bf914b786a97ccb612c1d4144d8.png)

*   Enter your name and email address. Leave the CA Email blank.
*   Select Saved to disk and hit continue. This will generate your `.certSigningRequest` file.

**To generate using OpenSSL:**

*   Generate a private RSA key file.

    $ openssl genrsa -out keyname.key 2048

*   Create the certificate signing request file by filling out the interactive form.

    $ openssl req -new -key keyname.key -out CertificateSigningRequest.certSigningRequest

#### Creating `.cer` file in Apple Developer Center[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#creating-cer-file-in-apple-developer-center "Direct link to creating-cer-file-in-apple-developer-center")

*   Navigate to [Certificates, IDs & Profiles › Certificates](https://developer.apple.com/account/resources/certificates/list)
     in the [Apple Developer Center](https://developer.apple.com/account)
     and create a new certificate. Select the certificate type, either iOS App Development or iOS Distribution.

![iOS Cert](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-profiles-ios-cert-1-ae6d0cc01b5bf534361a7f53abadc052.png)

*   Click 'Continue' and upload the `.certSigningRequest` you created. Then, download your certificate. It should be a `.cer` file.

#### Converting certificate file to `.p12`[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#converting-certificate-file-to-p12 "Direct link to converting-certificate-file-to-p12")

Next, we'll need to convert the certificate from a `.cer` file to a `.p12` file. It can be converted on Mac OSX by using Keychain Access and on other platforms by using OpenSSL.

**Using Keychain Access:**

*   Drag your `.cer` file into your login keychain.
*   Click the My Certificates tab.
*   Right-click the certificate and choose Export.

![iOS Cert](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-profiles-ios-cert-2-0d7f623c568a38fd7aa833edcfe2b776.png)

*   Select Personal Information Exchange (.p12) for File Format.
*   Save the certificate, giving it a strong password.

**Using OpenSSL:**

*   Download your iOS certificate to the same directory as your private RSA key.
*   Change the format of the iOS certificate to PEM.

    $ openssl x509 -inform DER -outform PEM -in ios_development.cer -out ios_development.cer.pem

*   Export the certificate as a `.p12` file, giving it a strong password.

    $ openssl pkcs12 -export -inkey keyname.key -in ios_development.cer.pem -out Certificates.p12

### Provisioning Profile[​](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#provisioning-profile "Direct link to Provisioning Profile")

Provisioning profiles give your app access to be installed, or _provisioned_, on specific devices. For iOS App Development or Ad Hoc Distribution provisioning profiles, devices are selected manually.

*   Navigate to [Certificates, IDs & Profiles › Profiles](https://developer.apple.com/account/resources/profiles/list)
     in the [Apple Developer Center](https://developer.apple.com/account)
     and create a new provisioning profile.

![iOS Provisioning Profile](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/assets/images/ss-profiles-ios-pp-1-c08ce1812e7d341729e1c8c4821c21ad.png)

*   Select the profile type based on your build type: iOS App Development, Ad Hoc, or App Store
*   Select the correct App ID (with the Bundle ID matching your app's `config.xml` file)
*   Select the certificate you generated
*   Select any and all devices if generating a development or ad hoc profile
*   Download the `.mobileprovision` file, which is your provisioning profile file

Contents
--------

*   [Overview](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#overview)
    
*   [Android Certificates](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#android-certificates)
    *   [Generating in Android Studio](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#generating-in-android-studio)
        
    *   [Generating with `keytool`](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#generating-with-keytool)
        
*   [iOS Certificates](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#ios-certificates)
    *   [Connecting Apple Developer Account to Xcode (optional)](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#connecting-apple-developer-account-to-xcode-optional)
        
    *   [Registering your App ID](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#registering-your-app-id)
        
    *   [Device Registration](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#device-registration)
        
    *   [Signing Certificate](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#signing-certificate)
        
    *   [Provisioning Profile](https://ionic.io/docs/appflow/package/credentials#provisioning-profile)
